overinflated sense of quality. remind employees frequently that work outcomes affect the quality of patient lives, mission driven. complexity is the rule! labyrinthine processes abound. as with other giant corporations, the employee experience is largely affected by local management, some of which can be petty and inconsistent. upper management likes to think they are developing a culture of quality, but mistakenly applies complexity instead of actual quality procedures. the result is majoring in the minors and minoring in the major aspects of designed quality. many enterprise resources are frustratingly difficult to work with and require dedicated support staff to police basic functionality. there are very few corporate perks, focusing instead on basic salary and an array of mostly unusable benefits ( e. g., cancer management health benefit ). pay and benefit package are generally competitive but do not expect any flexibility in package negotiation. the company is moving away from hybrid work environments with steadily - increasing demands for office attendance. this company is recovering ( 2024 ) from generally mismanaging supply - chain and quality - created problems of the covid years and will continue to have self - imposed financial headwinds for a few more years.

it was a wonderful experience to work at medtronic as it was the best place i ever worked. what is the best part of working at the company? work life balance and knowing that medtronic strives to be the best place to work and therefore medtronic was indeed the best placwe i ever worked what is the most stressful part about working at the company? the fact that our product goes inside a human body and needs to be perfect no parts bad in million or billion. what is the work environment and culture like at the company? it is mostly engineers and have highly educated employees which makes their culture very scientific what is a typical day like for you at the company? i would be working on projects, supervising equipment maintenance activities. work on continuous improvement and strategy to sustain it

no work life balance. no work life balance, high levels of micromanaging, no growth, shady practices you are expected to have no children at home, which is understandable, but what daycare is open until 7 : 30 pm? and no availability to change your shift after working here for years. micromanaging to the point your focus is on your metrics rather than actually producing quality work. good luck trying to move in any way, favoritism plays too heavily into your chances. misleading information on what your responsibilities will actually be when applying for the job and getting the approval letter.

good. overall it ' s a nice company to work but you have to deal with incompetent managers. they have no technical experience and very little knowledge about working process. colleagues are nice and very helpful.

you will be as successful as you choose to be. put in the time and effort. what is the best part of working at the company? best products on the market. across the specialties. what is the most stressful part about working at the company? work / life balance. you will be in demand. if not, you have to make that happen. what is the work environment and culture like at the company? work hard / smart and be rewarded. appreciate the talent / support around you what is a typical day like for you at the company? there isn t a typical day. best reward for add adults.
great place to work. competitive salaries and great products. executive leadership is good. innovative by way of acquiring new products and improving upon existing products.

team work and safety. schedule, teamwork, safety. favoritism, lack of transparency. great work conditions in terms of safety and work schedule. however, disappointment is due to favoritism and lack of transparency while selecting higher level employees.

high paced. a lot of great colleagues. a great early career role to learn about how to analyze competitors, markets, and investment opportunities. the corporate development group was fast paced and fun.

great job. love the job, once you get used to the schedule you will be able to thrive. a lot of extra pto offered. 12 hour shifts 3 days and then next week 4 days

a great place to work!! do self test on test equipment and make sure that test have passing self tests. the hardest part of the job, is when the techs didn ' t have parts to replace something that doesn ' t work on the programmer. workplace culture was good! the most enjoyable part of the job : the people, different cultures!
great place to work. no issues with the work and great people overall. large, mission driven company and strong supervision. i feel supported by the people i work with and by my supervisor.

good company. with medtronic being the size that it is, i definitely feel like they underpay their employees. 50 % 401k match up to 6 %. i liked my manager but upper management does not keep you in the loop

alright. this a good place to work to get quick money, the benefits are nice with tuition reimbursements. though they overwork you sometimes. management was bad during my second year in but it could be different for other shifts and work areas.

good place, get off the line if possible. it ' s a large company, opportunities come up relative often, you ' ll want to grab them and get off the line. pay is average, benefits are decent, they ' ll pay for your college degree in full for one of the areas / schools they selected
great company culture but lack of budget. company culture is great and everyone is really amazing to work with ; budgeting with projects is a bit tight which leads to some issues along the way.

great place to work. everyday is a bit different. sometimes the work load is spread very very thin, its okay tho. mind and body stimulation with challenges to stay on your feet and be an important member of the team providing valuable and essential technical and sales support

good consistency, poor management. high turnover due to lack of management or direction. it ' s a good starting point to get into the industry of medical sales. coworkers will make or break the experience.

working in mdt is not only a job to take care but a mission to continue and worth working for. what is the best part of working at the company? culture of belongingness where every one is welcome and vital to the organization. mdt is taking care of all the members whether you are in sales, marketing, leadership team or support, everyone is vital. yow will feel the harmony of the organization in general. little misunderstanding can easily be resolve and addressed with so much care of belongingness. what is the most stressful part about working at the company? waiting for allocation is quite stressful but with the proper messaging to the clients and key accounts, we can simply address the stress. we just need good flow of communication so that the clients will not be stressed too much for waiting and expecting the arrival of the stocks. what is the work environment and culture like at the company? it is like a family where everyone is welcomed and vital to the organization. what is a typical day like for you at the company? every day is a new day full of surprises and challenging endeavor yet very rewarding specially if you were able to alleviate the pain and extend life of the needy.

opportunities for growth. medtronic is a great company to start out at as an early - career engineer. lots of opportunities to get where you want as long as you leverage your network connections properly. can be frustrating at times but most days are enjoyable.

good company. medtronic makes great products with a lot of available resources and training to the do job right. can be high stress with a poor work / life balance, but that ' s not specific to just this company
great place. i enjoy going to work everyday. the support from management has been good. the mission is motivating. processes are a little cumbersome, but improving.

depends on the individual. well established company, good benefits, some management are the best in the business. lack of career growth opportunities, some management are unprofessional. when i first started with medtronic, i already had a good solid base of skills that supported my role. as the years past, i found myself stagnated and unable to look for mentorship and growth opportunities. in my case, my direct manager has been great. however, this is not the case in other areas or under different managers. the culture of management is more or less focused on the educational qualifications in lieu of practical, hands on experience which makes growing in a career path more often leading to end in dead - ends until you join the educational club, regardless of the prior knowledge you bring to the table. in my work, i am afforded a relaxing and laid back workflow, but after years of performing above expectation, growth and opportunities should not be off limits if ambition and drive is present.

best company ever. good pay rate. no cons at all. i worked at medtronic remotely, handling an 8 - hour shift each day. it was an incredible learning experience, collaborating with highly skilled and professional colleagues who fostered an environment for growth. i consistently gave my best, and my contributions were recognized by the company. given the opportunity, i would gladly join medtronic again to continue contributing to its success
great place to work, great atmosphere. a very nice place to work. supervisors and management are very nice and always helpful. very good canteen with wide variety of food. really good place to work.

great company. on the positive side, the job provides good hands - on experience in electrical assembly, and i had the opportunity to work with a team of skilled professionals. the company offers a structured

so far, so good! work - life balance, good coworkers, competitive salary. benefits are a little pricy. i ' m still fairly new, but so far everything is great. i ' ve already had time to expand my skills and learn new things. my coworkers are very kind and super helpful. work life balance is good, and the workload is reasonable.

i wouldn t go back or redo this opportunity given the opportunity. what is the best part of working at the company? micromanaged to the core night shift managers have favorites in which managers choose to turn the blind eye for how they like if they don t like you you ll always get in trouble and the opposite will be the outcome would be cool what is the most stressful part about working at the company? most stressful part about this company is you never know when you will get fired based off another s complaints. what is the work environment and culture like at the company? employees were cool most situations aroused with managers what is a typical day like for you at the company? long 12 hour days with no flexibility

great company with a great mission, and with amazing people. what is the best part of working at the company? its mission and focus on people what is the most stressful part about working at the company? staying abreast of key priorities across diverse stakeholder and businesses what is the work environment and culture like at the company? very inclusive and collaborative what is a typical day like for you at the company? i am not there anymore but when i was there it was fast paced, with some seasonality in firedrills around quarter and fy ends
great place to work. great place to work with good staff and structure processes. a lot of resources and quality strong culture. not micro management. fair payment and opportunities.

work life balance. medtronic is a great place to work. i ' ve been here for 10 + years. the professional growth is available if you want it. it is always room for improvement. you have to have the right mindset to get it done right the first time.

great if you want to work from home. overall not a bad place to work especially if you re just starting out. there is list of room for promotion especially if you have a degree. pros : work from home, coworkers and managers are great, good work life balance. cons : low pay, micromanaging

multicultural and fun. simple work. people are very friendly, they ' re very organized, management is great to deal with. the work - life balance is also respected and there job is not overly complicated with crazy deadlines or kpis.

great place to work. free coffee, good culture, benefits and pay. long hours, work life balance might be difficult to manage. i had a chance to work with a diverse team from different regions and backgrounds. i was able to compare different processes and requirements per region. maintaining work - life balance is a challenge if you are supporting a globally diverse team and process
good company and good benefits. good company and good benefits. people stay for long periods as there is a clear sense of belonging. a lot of changes in structure and directions is the con.

medtronic employment. helping patients enrolled in the clinical trials & relationships with their providers. yearly layoffs, turnovers / changes in management. * entering clinical data, running reports, payments * being able to multitask due to daily competing demands * management ( turnovers, changes, communication ) * interacting with coworkers seemed better working conditions until covid and everyone went remote missing the day - to - day interaction * yearly layoffs * helping patients to alleviate pain for a better life by conducting clinical trials

oppressive bureaucracy. work life balance. oppressive woke bureaucracy. i did like some things about medtronic. they allowed me to be pretty flexible with my schedule. and i become really good friends with the people i directly worked with. however, the problem with them is more in the disgusting amount of beuaracy and politics in everything they do. i watched them throw millions of dollars down the drain for the dumbest things, then they lay people off because they poorly managed their money. it is such a beaucratic dump too. they world rather you sit around and do nothing than work because it doesn ' t follow their process of how they like things to be done. they are also an extremely " woke " company. shoving it ' s ideology down your throat whenever they get a chance. and just like big pharma, they prey on people ' s illnesses. in one of their town halls, they mentioned that their profits were down 10 % because people were healthier that year, and were upset because of it.

slow learning and very little team cohesion. the rate at which i developed new skills at this position was a small fraction of most other places i ' ve worked. it ' s partially red tape and med device paper work, but also due to most of the work being outsourced to automation vendors ( who will inevitably be blamed when things go wrong ). team cohesion is non - existent. you ' re essentially on your own. collaboration and mentorship is just not part of the culture here.

deceiving work environment. good work - life balance, fair compensation, interesting projects. very poor management, senior leaders cannot be trusted, extremely cliquey environment, a lot of nepotism and favoritism, under funded programs, poor decision making, rushes to put out technology, racism and sexism. this company has an amazing reputation for being a great place to work, but i would argue that can be location dependent. would not recommend company.

unorganized business. immediate use of 40 hours of pto. management is terrible at this location! so unorganized. this place is a joke. there is no communication amongst the supervisors and workers. lack of trainers. no knowledge of how to actually run a clean room nor a material handlers position. they will hire anyone. lack of materials and laptops. if you get injured on the job, don ' t worry, they will take it out of your pto. they do not pay for your injuries. it ' s just a bunch of bull. i ' ve never seen a company that doesn ' t know how to run the business or handle their employees. i will not recommend this place to anyone i know.

fast pace. i would not say that i like working here but i like how theyre making device to save people, just like the rest of the people in the world we need job to live. i like getting things done and work hard.
okay place changing directions. as long as you keep doing your work, you are fine. management is changing directions and engineering is under pressure to deliver. market is competitive.

great place to work - sometimes. worked there for approximately 15 years. great when i started back in 2000 but as the years progressed, the environment changed. so much management but not a lot of great managing. support wasn ' t always there and the moral dropped significantly in the few years before i left due to changing workplace culture, layoffs, and lack of transparency. excellent benefits and pay.

soulless job. pays the bills. little room to advance. your typical 9 - 5 soulless job, with ocassional pizza days because they are family. if you want to work here, you probably need to find a staffing agency because they didn ' t hire directly. at least when i was working there
great company. great company with excellent benefits and leadership. top quality products, first in class. great size territory, and an amazing reputation. i throughly enjoyed working here.

unpleasant, stressful and underpaid. i worked at this company for years and overall, i would not recommend it to others. the pay was not competitive for the amount of work expected, and the pto was just okay. the benefits were decent, but that ' s about the only positive thing i can say. one of the biggest issues i had was the extreme micromanagement. the supervisors were mostly young and inexperienced, which led to poor management skills and a lack of support for their employees. additionally, i had concerns about the ethics of the company, there were instances where i felt uncomfortable with the practices being used and it made me question the overall integrity of the company. if you ' re okay with being overworked, underpaid, and micromanaged, then maybe it could be a good fit for you. but for me, it was a stressful and unpleasant experience.

interesting. working at medtronic in heerlen was a mixed experience. while the company has a strong mission and some great people, frequent mismanagement and poor leadership decisions often created unnecessary stress and inefficiencies. unfortunately, this made it not the best place to work in the long run

not bad. pay is garbage for what ' s required, leadership cares about the bottom line only, and everything feels forced and not genuine. can be a to performer but get an average yearly rating, or below average, bc the site didn ' t perform well as a while, even though you didn ' t have anything to do with most of the metrics

good pay conditions tho boring work. i would recommend it to people if they are able to put up with montonay of job. nice people to work for, good pay and work life balance, career opportunities. only downside very boring work.

great product. medtronic diabetes makes the most advanced product to manage type 1 diabetes. always striving to improve product and services. pockets of management practice micromanagement preventing growth of team members and adding unnecessary stress to team members.

great company culture. i had a great internship, colleagues are friendly and i was able to participate in many continuous improvement projects. however, sometimes days were filled with meetings that weren ' t very value adding

not the same company as it once was. i feel like they could turn it around with the new cfo but leadership is heavily focused on cutting expense to hit bottom line versus driving innovation like it once was. doesn ' t feel like the same company. they ' re losing a lot of great talent either through rifs or attrition. hopefully they can turn it around and get back on track but rifs are making it hard to do 3 or 4 people ' s jobs just to get the work done. love the people. love the mission. need to get back to those being the focus.

culture has changed. it has been an increasingly difficult to feel gratified in my job here because of dealing with constant rifs and budget cuts. i know it has been tough since covid, but it seems like a different company, and not in a good way.

productive and fun in working. what is the best part of working at the company? good environment for work collegues and team leaders and supervisors are very gpod what is the most stressful part about working at the company? inever feel any stress in this job

made me realize i was just wasting my potential and time. management, overtime, work culture, people, pay. when i came onto the scene, i was pretty much thrown into the mess. the management was very poor. we would receive a new supervisor every year and it would feel like things would never get finished due to the constant change of heads. the constant mandatory overtime pretty much drained the life and soul out of everyone and by the end of it, to celebrate all your hard work for those many overtime hours is a pizza party while your superiors and corporate gets all the credit and bonuses from it. yeah, it s insulting the more you think about it. no good deed here ever goes unpunished. a technician / engineer got upset with me because i told him he had to have ppe on in the dry / clean room because he didn t have any on and wasn t willing to go look for some in the other dry rooms. i ended up letting him do his thing because he threatened to not fix what he was supposed to be there to fix. was just trying to look out for another employee. as of now, i ve changed careers and i have a much better work life balance than i once did when i worked here. in conclusion, you re better off pursuing what you re passionate about than wasting it on an assembler position at medtronic that sucks the life out of you.

great job. the job was great because i got to do it from home. the experience and the company itself was a great opportunity for me. i would recommend anyone to go and work with the team at medtronic.

good place to work. mostly easy job with great people. great facility and management was good, always had your back and willing to work with you. pay was ok for the job and tended to increase much more frequently than most manufacturing places. the training is great, they don ' t just throw you in there and tell you to read the work instructions like other places will.

non - existent work - life balance. work life balance is nearly non - existent, especially if you joined at a facility that is not performing. unreal expectations and timelines that affect work life balance.

stretch your capacity and work with incredibly talented individuals. incredibly smart and strategic people. you are a number, and this company doesn ' t owe you anything at the end of the day. as a district manager, my number one priority was revenue and my team. i was the internal voice for the reps working hard in their accounts. i was responsible for advocating for their needs and ensuring internal resources were directed appropriately so that they could show up as their best selves. i learned how to navigate ambiguous situations, advocate loudly for needs, and win with a team. the hardest part of this role is finding your voice and making your needs known in an extremely fast and complex environment inside medtronic. the best part was all of the people i got to work alongside to tackle some very tough problems.

lots of nice people good canteen. good benefits if you manage to get a contract. poor communication from management can be toxic environment work is repetitive but easy no support or proper training team leaders do a lot of sitting and playing cards in building 2 evening shift not a good environment

a good platform for new graduate. professional. too many employee with too few star products. i joined medtronic as a trainee and left as a sr. specialist. and medtronic itself does match the name of top 1 medical device company. i learned a lot from my mentor, manager and skip manager. they are nice and professional. i ' m grateful for the opportunity to work there.
fun place to work and learn. lot of support from team exposure to new knowledge flexible work schedule support carrier progression excellent benefits like insurance cycle to work salary sacrifice
great. you will love it! great people great organization. they care about you wnd your career. the career development options and movement upwards is really good.

excellent opportunity to excel in many different career options. what is the best part of working at the company? restores quality of life through the technology used within this company. benefits are excellent. what is the most stressful part about working at the company? meeting hyper sensitive deadlines what is the work environment and culture like at the company? diverse with open door policy among management.
culture. lots of leadership in the past 3 years have caused a number of re - orgs and job loss. lots of disgruntled employees and lost trust in leadership. they are only out for themselves.

greed. this company can be a great place to work if you start in a management position. however, if you ' re starting at the bottom, be prepared for a rough experience. the company tends to exploit those with a strong work ethic, pushing you to go above and beyond without proper support or recognition. work - life balance is virtually nonexistent. even when severely understaffed, you ' re still expected to meet if not exceed unrealistic performance goals. upper management often reacts to problems instead of proactively addressing them, and they consistently refuse to hire additional help, even during profitable fiscal years. if you re just looking for a job, this place might do for the short term. but if you value your time, health, and career growth, don t stay too long. know your worth and move on when the time is right.
positive. great company to work. great culture, great people, great work - life balance, great compensation, definitely recommend others. excellent company to work for.

some departments better than others. little work life balance unless you ' re in r & d. operations and manufacturing were forced to stay late and come in on weekends often, and there was backlash if you maintained boundaries and were unavailable outside of 8 - 5 m - f. many are also not receptive to changing the ways after many years of doing things a certain way, definitely not the place where innovation is appreciated.

it s fair. good benefits. they like to downsize. 12 hour days, 3days one week then the other week will be 4. usually there is overtime. very detailed oriented work environment. if that s not something you can do it will be miserable for not only you but the rest of the team

nice people, poor management. fun events, beautiful campus. no job security. cas is not going to do well if they don ' t do something about the poor management. going above and beyond means nothing to the executives, they just expect more. there is a very distinct line between the haves and the have nots. the lower level employees are expected to return to office, but not upper management. they can live and work anywhere and never need to travel into headquarters. a lot of travel and hiring freezes, tons of turnover at every level. more than i ' ve ever seen in over 20 years. it ' s cheaper just to hire and replace contractors. the people who work there are very friendly, i saw very little ego or unpleasantness. they do host a lot of fun events, and it seems as though they are trying to build a culture there again.

fun work environment. it s a fun working environment. i ve been working here for the past five years and i love it. everyone is so welcome. i highly recommend it. great teamwork great communication.

worst please to work at. no pros. everything. the work culture is horrible. they don ' t care about you at all they only care about if you did your job and that ' s it. a typical day would be going to work a 6 am till 6 pm and the management up you butt for you to work nonstop. work culture is toxic. the only somewhat enjoyable was some of the coworkers.

pressure, motivation, and progress. large career development space arrange work processes in an orderly manner international companies with good cross - cultural collaboration work in an orderly and planned manner. according to the workflow, multiple departments collaborate and negotiate to reach a unified opinion as soon as possible and put it into practice.

indifferent in the department i worked within, but ok with it. flexible schedule ; remote work. management techniques were outdated, staff lacked basic skillsets and were overpaid. came in as a quality program manager to manage supplier eu mdr compliance across all business units. the team of managers used an overly complex excel spreadsheet to manage and track status, compliance, and closure. i finished my job too early and stayed on payroll doing internal quality system audits until the contract ended. not an ideal role or situation.

good clinical work, tech and management lost in the blue. the common theme at medtronic is leading with the brand name and giving out rebates instead of updating the technology and providing best in class service to remain competitive. clinical specialists are basically treated like fill in people. yet for an entry level job it s a good step into medical device.
fun place. work was easy and plentiful. people were really nice and there was a lot of chance for growth. opportunities for overtime if you want it and the supervisors were really nice

pros and cons. great team and colleagues. say / do from management can seem a bit two - faced sometimes. concerned about lack of sustainable company valuation growth and when the board will meaningfully act.

flexible. working at medtronic has been a rewarding experience overall, with a strong sense of purpose behind the work. the company s mission to improve patients lives really comes through in the culture, and it s motivating to know that the projects you contribute to have a direct impact on health outcomes. medtronic provides good benefits, competitive pay, and solid opportunities for professional development. there are structured programs for training, tuition assistance, and career growth, which make it a good place to build long - term skills. on the positive side, colleagues are collaborative and supportive, and leadership generally emphasizes diversity and inclusion. work - life balance is reasonable in most departments, with flexible schedules available in many roles. the company is large, so there are chances to move internally and explore different areas if you want to grow.

depends on manager. many diverse employee resource groups, friendly people, great newly renovated office building. hybrid and global teams, lack of management support, huge middle management structure. typical work included project support for new product development, review quality risk documents, and assessing complaints / issues for quality risk. role is primarily to ensure quality risk is prioritized within the teams, pretty basic. the broader organizational unit is very encouraging and uplifting. often, opportunities in other groups are open to all, with the caveat that participation depends on the manager. some managers are better at supporting staff in their career ambitions outside of the team than others. many teams in this office are also split up between offices in connecticut, colorado, and germany. this can limit team bonding and, i found, hinder productive collaboration. in addition, 3 days mandatory in office was required but in person collaboration was rare across project teams and functional groups.

would not recommend working here. irish are outnumbered. people being hired that can ' t speak basic english. alot of politics at work. i work nights and the wages are not enough to match the cost of living. company always going against anything that employees would benefit from. there is no nice working culture in this company

went out of business. none. too many to list. not much to say except a very negative experience. at the beginning not much help in getting started. no list of clients. no training on using the work order process. minimal training

don t expect raises to even match inflation. overall a boring, dead end job. no room for advancement and the churn amongst employees is the highest i ve ever seen. a miserable place to work with no company culture. in the year and a half i was here they would not give raises that even matched the increase in inflation so the longer you stay here the less money you make in the long run. pay was less than entry level positions at mcdonalds.

nothing good to say. this place and its management are soulless and soul - sucking. employees have no value or significance as human beings. you are nothing more than a cog in the machine. the unofficial motto is profits over people.
great place to work. great colleagues. nice office with free tea, coffee and fresh fruit. hybrid working. training and onboarding could be greatly improved - but overall recommended

gsk walks its talk - it ' s the real deal. supportive and collaborative culture. minimal control of sales performance. as a pharma sales rep your day consists of pre - call planning for each of the providers you ' ll be calling on in order to ensure messaging is consistent with what your behavior change ask is from the provider. typically a rep hosts a lunch at a provider - 3 to 4 days a week. sometimes 5. in many cases when calling on a doctor at a practice you ' re unable to see them as they ' re with patients. your days can be like groundhog day. " do you need any samples "... over and over. saying the same thing over and over. i ' ve learned that i should have started this job when i was younger. there are many long - time sales reps. 20 years plus is common. i don ' t know how they still like what they do. it is boring and repetitive. the workplace culture is supportive and collaborative. anytime i ' ve needed assistance or guidance, others are willing to help and share their knowledge. the hardest part of the job is the multiple metrics and measurements. it ' s practically impossible to balance all the different kpi ' s we ' re required to hit. we ' re told it ' s not box checking to hit them. the truth is that they are. i believe they detract from what we actually should be doing each day. in addition, the level of control i believe i have in changing provider behavior is limited. so - the hardest part overall is the lack of control of the numbers and the fact that you can do everything you possibly can to grow the business and still come up empty. it ' s defeating. the most enjoyable part of the job is talking to the providers in the offices and building relationships with their team. i ' ve met so many different people from different cultures and countries. i find it fascinating to learn about them, what they do, and why they do it. it ' s the people in the practices that bring me the most enjoyment.

good place to work, if you can navigate the subpar site management team. open office ; good salary ; good benefits ; prestige of working for a major pharmaceutical company. disconnected upper management, ineffective hr department, lack of efficient planning for major projects. in a typical day, most of your time is spent in the lab analyzing and running tests on product samples. when not in the lab, you ' ll be analyzing data and generating official reports for each of the many product samples you test. downtime during the day is usually spent completing assigned laboratory duties ( i. e. cleaning water baths, refilling incubators, standardizing ph meters, etc. ). while in this role, i learned a lot about how to view the biotech manufacturing process through the lens of quality. the immediate management team was quite professional and very helpful, but many of the higher ups at the director - level seemed disconnected and generally uninterested with the concerns of the rank and file manufacturing or analytical worker. this helped to create a somewhat tense workplace culture, where those operating the manufacturing machinery or running the analytical tests often felt unheard by the upper management. the most difficult part of the job for me was trying to serve as a somewhat effective intermediary between the rank and file and upper management. the most enjoyable part of the job was having the chance to interact with so many different departments almost at will ( due to the open office design ).

a challenging yet valuable experience that built my resilience and highlighted the importance of supportive leadership and fairness. what is the best part of working at the company? the best part of working at the company was the structured processes and employee benefits, which initially reflected the standards of a well - established multinational organization. these aspects created a positive first impression and provided a solid foundation to carry out my responsibilities effectively. while there were internal challenges later on, the experience ultimately helped me grow professionally and deepened my appreciation for fair leadership, transparent recognition, and a truly supportive work culture. what is the most stressful part about working at the company? the most stressful aspect of working at the company was navigating internal team dynamics and the lack of consistent support from management. despite consistently delivering strong performance, recognition was often redirected due to internal politics. i was also restricted from taking my entitled annual leave, which added to the pressure and affected work - life balance. ultimately, my employment was abruptly terminated, seemingly as a means for my manager to protect her own position. while this outcome was disappointing, the overall experience taught me valuable lessons about professional resilience, the importance of ethical leadership, and the value of working in an environment that fosters transparency and mutual respect. what is the work environment and culture like at the company? the company initially presented a structured and process - driven environment, with benefits and systems that reflected a well - organized workplace. however, over time, i observed that the actual work culture varied across teams. in my experience, there was a lack of consistent managerial support and transparency, which impacted collaboration and recognition. internal politics and unclear communication created challenges that affected team morale. while not all teams may have had the same experience, i believe the culture could benefit from stronger leadership, fairer recognition practices, and a more supportive and inclusive approach to employee well - being. what is a typical day like for you at the company? a typical day involved managing assigned tasks independently, often taking on responsibilities similar to those of an engineer. i stayed focused on meeting deadlines, maintaining quality standards, and supporting team objectives. while the work itself was engaging and offered opportunities to apply my skills, there was limited collaboration or guidance from management, which sometimes made the workload feel isolating. despite these challenges, i remained committed to delivering consistent results and ensuring my tasks were completed efficiently.

balanced but challenging experience at honeywell. i appreciated working with many kind, intelligent, and driven colleagues. the environment encouraged collaboration, and i was fortunate to learn from cross - functional teams. a typical day involved managing multiple priorities under tight timelines, often requiring self - direction as management provided general guidance but expected individuals to figure out the details independently. while the company values performance and efficiency, the workload could be overwhelming, especially on a weekly cadence where expectations rarely aligned with the time available. success here often meant being available well beyond standard hours 60 + hour weeks were not unusual. my biggest challenge was the lack of visibility into how my contributions tied directly to larger corporate strategies, as tasks were often so segmented that the broader impact felt disconnected. for those who thrive in high - demand environments and prioritize work as a central part of life, honeywell may be a strong fit. however, for those seeking work - life balance and clearer alignment between effort and strategic outcomes, it may be more difficult to sustain long term

when all they have to do is tell you in person. however management doesn ' t want to be seen conversing with production operators so they don ' t hurt their image, they send their puppets to do it. the most enjoyable part of the job, best part of day is leaving the plant and not having to think about that place till next shift. only reason i ' m still there is because compensation.

hiring process is a bit on the longer side and varies, personally it took about a month for me, for a few other coworkers i was hearing up to 2 - 3 months before they were finally given job offers. generally there was only 1 mandatory meeting per day with all staff on the shift. some safety tips, general idea of productivity and occasionally a hint of potential mandatory overtime. depending on any incidents that happen, there may be another meeting thats mandatory for the entire building, not just the shift, explaining what happened and giving advice on how to avoid it in the future without naming names of any employees involved. overtime is ridiculous, esp at dk6, you will probably be looking at 50 - 80hrs / wk for the entire duration of your employment. dk1, dk4 and dk5 are a lot more relaxed on the overtime, but make up for it with having far tighter productivity / performance metrics to meet. your safety is everything, but if you fail to meet productivity / performance metrics, you ' ll get disciplinary actions 2 - 3 times up to and including termination. most management above your immediate supervisor has no clue how your job works and doesn ' t care, my previous supervisor was amazing however and everyone sort of lost hope after he moved on to a different company. as with most jobs, the coworkers are both the best and second worst part of the job, met a lot of really awesome people and a few incredibly irritating people while working here. personally, i ' d go back if the overtime wasn ' t as heavily mandatory and the performance metrics weren ' t near impossible to meet. its a bit hard to keep up 100 % work performance when you have 0 - 1 day off a week and are expected to move at a speed of 300 + cases per hour. it ' s up to you to manage yourself while cap takes a cut of your hard earned work. state of the art backpack provided. they will not provided assistance in finding new contracts. my experience working with capgemini in redmond was deeply frustrating and disappointing. if you re considering contracting with them especially for tech projects be warned. when the client project suddenly ended, capgemini had absolutely no plan for what would come next. my entire team was put " on the bench, " and despite their claims of supporting contractors between projects, they put zero effort into helping us find new work. it was a complete facade, just a cycle of unresponsiveness, unanswered emails, and vague, empty promises. there was no transparency about layoffs. people were quietly let go without any real communication or warning. the pay was also far below market average for the role, and the pto offered was barely usable just enough to say it exists on paper. when you try to get answers about work, hr, benefits, anything, no one responds. emails, teams messages, phone calls... all ignored. it felt like we were ghosted the moment the project ended with 0 direction on what to do next. in short, working with capgemini was a cycle of shady practices, poor pay, and complete lack of support. avoid them if you value your time, career, and peace of mind.

positive growth, culture quirk. overall, a great place to work and grow your it career. opportunities abound, mobility is encouraged ( though sometimes finding internal opportunities is challenging ), and there is plenty of opportunity to work with interesting clients seeking to push the bounds of it ( especially ai ). the company does have a few culture " quirks, " primarily based upon the employee community ' s obsession with job title and grade. this is promoted by a leadership belief that the highest grade people must the most technically or operationally knowledgeable. many team members believe that promotions should be seniority based ( they are performance and need based, that is, does the company need a higher graded resource and has a resource performed at the needed level? ) ; also, on occasion there is a deferential team behavior. that is, the highest grade person at the table must always be right. however, this can be overcome through positive collaboration and inclusive practices. asking the quiet person at the table what they think, or asking a stakeholder questions about their positions, are generally welcomed and lead to better performance.

watch your back because some of employees will do you wrong. what is the best part of working at the company? didn t mine the work, but they don t listen to a temp, especially when there full time employee did the mistake. made it look like the temp did it. just to cover his butt. what is the most stressful part about working at the company? when you get a fingerprint at something that you didn t even do.. what is the work environment and culture like at the company? it was fine. i have not much more to say because know one want to listen to a temp. because employees at backer tilly just cover themselves and they close you out and make you feel like you don t belong. what is a typical day like for you at the company? keep your self busy and done correct.

amazing work life balance, decent pay. this is my first job out of college and the work life balance for me is amazing while i ' m working at home. got offered less then what the hr rep told me was the starting salary, considering i had no other offers at the time i didn ' t have much to argue about it.
good benefits. baker tilly has very good benefits and above average number of paid holidays. large organization and needs to listen to and trust their employees.

motivative and fun work place, great bonuses and incentives. what is the best part of working at the company? baker tilly is a work with your schedule type of company. great bonus incentives. what is the most stressful part about working at the company? none so far. i ' ve been stress free. what is the work environment and culture like at the company? the work environment is diverse and friendly. what is a typical day like for you at the company? a typical day for me is smooth and easy. i love this type of work.
nice area to work. most colleague and managements are nice and helpful, they are happy to answer the question and willing to help and have a discussion if staff face any difficulty.
professional. great training program. reasonable deadlines and expectations. work on your own schedule. supervisors are always available to assist with questions or concerns.

wonderful experience interacting with customers, other tax experts and supervisors and learning as i go. what is the best part of working at the company? learning from fellow tax experts and they learning from me about assisting customers. what is the most stressful part about working at the company? some customers may be impatient and want an answer right away. also if they ' re not getting a deduction or credit they ' re looking for, they may take it out on you. what is the work environment and culture like at the company? very friendly and interactive. fellow experts, supervisors and managers share their questions and day to day experiences. what is a typical day like for you at the company? log on. sign in and make myself available for turbotax customer calls and chats. if i can ' t answer a question right away i research on either the irs website the turbotax website or google. if still i don ' t get an answer i asked a lead supervisor and while waiting on their answer do further research. give the answer to the customer or walk the customer through. during our near the end of the call or chat take notes down. ask the customer if there is anything else he or she needs help with. at the end of the call sank the customer for calling and tell them about filling out the survey about their experience with me. between calls check email to order researcher training. near the end of the day to follow up call or chats if any.
work life balance. no ot was required friendly colleagues on job training available clear career path. helpful for you if you would get started in professional field. valuable working experience
fun. provides good plateform to become more professional and ethical in our work and enables me to be more update with technical standards and keep me dedicated and committed with work

i liked it. it was a great firm to work for but i have heard it really matters what team you are on. mine was run very well and everything was organized so my hours stayed consistent but others were more chaotic and last minute people would have to work all weekend to catch up.
great place to work. long hours but great colleagues and managers were very supportive and would teach anything that was required. not much incentives but pay was decent.

got to big for itself. company recently merged with multiple firms. seemed to be positive at first but turned out to be broken promises after the former ceo left. unorganized, understaffed ( still had a layoff ) and lack of training. better to work at a big 4 that has structure.

i m sad i worked for a company that prioritizes the bottom line over its employees. what is the best part of working at the company? they offer unlimited pto, however actually getting pto approved is almost impossible what is the most stressful part about working at the company? this is a very stressful company to work for. you are a number with charge hour goals that must be met. they expect at least 65 hours of work each week what is the work environment and culture like at the company? the culture baker tilly promotes is completely different than the actual work environment. you are a number and if anything gets in the way of your productivity you are pushed out what is a typical day like for you at the company? very stressful. the training is horrible. you are pretty much thrown into the storm with no life jacket
good colleagues. co - workers were, for the most part, smart, dedicated, and generous with their time. it support for remote workers was responsive. good resources for complex situations.

great place to start your career. loved starting my career here. big enough company where you get lots of different opportunities to grow, but small enough that you are not just a face in the crowd. if you are open to working long hours, the opportunities are endless and are a great launch pad when you are ready to leave public accounting.

good people, good culture, bad management. stay away from them. flexible hours, hybrid schedule, decent benefits. no guidance, no structure, no one to trust. it ' s sad that they place bottom line results over good people. they grew too fast too soon and their left hand did not know what their right hand was doing. overall, i regret going there.

it started as a great place to work that quickly 180 ' d the moment the new ceo, jeff ferro took over. what is the best part of working at the company? the good things are gone. this used to be a company dedicated to their employees. now it ' s just like every other major corporation. no more work from home, terrible benefits, and low pay. what is the most stressful part about working at the company? every day is a brand new problem. you will gain many responsibilities, but you will not be compensated in any way for it. the work you eill be doing, you ' ll be paid less than half for and you will never get a title advancement. what is the work environment and culture like at the company? toxic. it ' s extremely cut - throat, with management more worried about how they look rather than how employees are treated. what is a typical day like for you at the company? you will come in looking to do work that was piled onto your plate the day before, followed by a new massive amount of work piled on top. everything is important, which means nothing is.
enjoyable experience. fun and friendly people, great environment to be a part of. received assistance when needed and learned how to prepare tax returns for corporate entities

unfortunate. what is the best part of working at the company? the work was interesting and fun what is the most stressful part about working at the company? the payroll manager and supervisor were incompetent what is the work environment and culture like at the company? the culture was dictated but never followed through on. complete scam. not family friendly what is a typical day like for you at the company? payroll processing for 6500 employees

good. good and good and good i mean good and even really good cause when it s goof it s good. sometimes better, sometimes good but ultimately good. thanks for attentiin

scope for growth. bakertilly in dubai is the company for you if you are at your initial stage of career and looking for a career progression. managers and partners are good. management is old style. needs improvement in terms of work culture and employee benefits.

great people, depends on your team. most of the people i ve come across are great, but my department doesn t win good jobs. would probably be happier on another team. also, there have been lot of recent changes from up top that weren t communicated in the best way, such as a 180 on the view on rto.
good culture. can achieve work life balance. moderate pay. has some outstation opportunities. can know a lot of friends. some managers are nice to work with. good location.

strange managing partner. what is the best part of working at the company? like the two best days of buying a boat what is the most stressful part about working at the company? managing partner and leadership what is the work environment and culture like at the company? just lack of leadership creates issues. what is a typical day like for you at the company? busy season - forget it - 7 days

rewarding job. what is the best part of working at the company? the management support is amazing. the training is great. the team work is amazing what is the most stressful part about working at the company? to schedule the work hours 3 months before. what is the work environment and culture like at the company? excellent work environment and culture. everyone is very professional and friendly. what is a typical day like for you at the company? it is only 4 hours which goes fast.

interesting opportunity, horrible pay structure and reconciliation for loss time and wages. partnering with intuit - turbo tax live,, you are assigned in to typical pods, the usual slack channel, and then it goes hay wire. between the compliance and regualtion for the main company you must go through, and the refresher course provided by intuit every year, you are only paid for half of the training hours it actually take. there is a heiarchy of who actually gets assigned actual clients and who actually is given the opportunity to do their job. - - - thousands of us had issues with payroll. and they eliminated the payroll department so that you could never hold anyone accountable and created several different conditions of pay. - - - lowest earning tax season on record, i ' ve been preparing taxes every year for over 10 years.

i really enjoyed working for this company as a remote tax preparer. work from home. it is seasonal. i have learned so much in this past tax season, management was always great lots of assistance if any questions come up i really appreciate the opportunity to work from home.

work is rewarding but pay is abysmal. rewarding, stellar benefits. busy season sucks, pay is well below what it should be. low pay, long hours, and high expecations. nothing out of the ordinary for public accounting but its not like baker tilly is reinventing the wheel or looking to improve lifestyle. if youre a well performing employee expect to get ran into the ground. work itself is rewarding despite the rigor and you have great upward mobility.

flexibility, but lacks a strong culture and cohesiveness. remote and hybrid work options. lack of strong vision and culture. pros : flexibility to work remote / hybrid, schedule personal appt ' s if needed during the day. i view the open pto policy as a con, i ' d rather have a vacation allotment, i take less vacation here than i did at my prior firm because of it. i sense push back on taking time whereas when i had a bucket it was expected people would use it. the benefits are decent and i at least have a team that cares and who are good at what they do. not all teams and leaders are created equal. i am surprised for a firm their size how low tech they are. they operate lean in a lot of areas that you would expect a firm of their size would have more established processes and structure for. with the new private equity buy - in there does seem to be a waning sense of job security. while some offices or teams perhaps have an established culture, the firm overall has a weak culture and not a clearly defined vision. whether you loved alan whitman or hated him, he at least had a defining vision for the firm that is lacking now. it ' s not a horrible place to work, but could make improvements that would make it that much better.

flexible schedule, overtime opportunity, remote work setting, region specific work load. flexible schedule, no micro - managing, pay. not many long term opportunities for non - credentialed persons, no incentives. i appreciate the fully remote setting, the flexible schedule, and the fact that you are assigned to a specific region. baker tilly is a global company, being assigned to a specific regions eliminates confusion when it comes to state specific tax laws. i also appreciate the team culture, every one assists when it comes to needing help. you are provided with plenty of resources to get the job done. the pay is good too!

great company - but it is public accounting. great staff, good atmosphere, remote work possible, many different types of files and you get to see it all. long hours as expected in public accounting. great company to work at, went from co - op to assistant manager. the hours are a lot, but that is expected in public accounting. great people and mostly great clients. overall a great company to work at. has events for staff, talks with staff if they are not working at the expected skill level and gives feedback on every file.

not excited and sometimes burn out. as an audit associate at a public accounting firm, the work was steady and provided solid experience, though it often felt routine and lacked excitement.

productive, educative and well compensated. what is the best part of working at the company? flexibility on schedules, pay rates what is the most stressful part about working at the company? some customers can be rude and disrespectful when frustrated.

overworked, underpaid, have to be willing to work extreme hours for promotion. pros : there are some solid coworkers cons : everything else. the firm is a disaster. they continuously bicker over processes and policies, of which none are clear or ever the same person to person. much of management is in a mood half the time, and overall not capable of managing resulting in blaming staff below them for inefficiencies. the pay is low and hours long. there ' s always some new flashy program or policy they are trying to implement which has no real meaning or benefit. if you want to grow into a quality accountant avoid this firm.

okay, struggling with trying to keep " smaller " firm culture while pursuing top 10 status. our former firm merged into baker tilly two years ago. i think at one time bt had more of a smaller firm feel while offering some perks of a larger firm, internal training, mktg presence, etc. but their goal is really to be a 5 billion $ firm and with that and all the incoming mergers, the focus is primarily on utilization. it ' s not a horrible place to work, but i personally struggle with the lack of mission and purpose at the national level, there really is not a defined culture. there are good people in the seattle office but being on the west coast you are not in the mainstream with other employees in the mid - west and east coast where the number of employees is more highly concentrated. i think they also struggle with understanding salaries in more high cost of living areas. the seattle office prior to merger had an atmosphere of high transparency, which is not the case with baker tilly. expectations are not always clear at the national level vs. the local level, low autonomy compared to what we used to enjoy. wouldn ' t not recommend, but may not be a long term option for some. public accounting in general can be tough, with a continued focus on utilization things may go down a path that won ' t work for some.

no career improvement, hard to have promotion. working in an environment where there is no career improvement and promotions are hard to come by can be quite challenging. it feels like being stuck in a perpetual cycle of monotony, with no room for growth or advancement. the lack of opportunities for promotion not only hampers professional development but also affects motivation and job satisfaction. it s disheartening to see your skills and talents go unrecognized, and your aspirations unfulfilled. this situation can lead to a decrease in productivity and morale over time. employers should strive to create a workplace that encourages career progression and recognizes employees efforts. after all, a motivated and satisfied workforce is key to the success of any organization. in conclusion, a stagnant career path and limited promotion opportunities can significantly impact an employee s motivation, satisfaction, and overall performance. it s crucial for organizations to address these issues to retain talent and ensure their growth.

micromanaged and lacking constructive feedback. none. work - from - home arrangements were revoked without any explanation, accompanied by threats regarding job security. the work environment was stressful due to micromanagement, lack of constructive feedback, and misuse of authority. job security was often used as leverage in conversations, making the work environment feel unstable and pressured. advice for management. invest in leadership training manager should undergo proper training on effective leadership, communication, and employee management. decision - making should be based on logic and professionalism rather than personal emotions. trust your employees reduce micromanagement and allow more autonomy in daily tasks. employees perform better when they feel trusted and empowered. provide clear and constructive feedback instead of just pointing out mistakes, offer guidance on how employees can improve. regular, meaningful feedback can boost morale and productivity. ensure consistency in policies work - from - home arrangements and job expectations should be communicated transparently and applied fairly to avoid confusion and favoritism. the manager demonstrated clear favoritism, for example, roses were given only to their preferred employee in front of others who did not receive any. foster a supportive and professional work culture avoid using job security as leverage in conversations. a stable, respectful environment will encourage employees to stay and contribute positively.

good place to work and people focused, but can be difficult to progress. before i joined ey, i ' d heard lots of things about poor work life balance, being overworked etc and so i was not sure i was making the right decision joining. i have been here 3 years ( technology consulting ) and i can say that it is not a bad place to work for at all - lots of flexibility around working from home which to me is the most important. you are able to manage your workload, and there ' s a lot of support around. however, here are the things that you need to be mindful of : - your counsellor and the team you are part of will define your experience at ey. if you land in a toxic team, or with a bad counsellor, change immediately. when you hear people saying they loved / hated working at ey, this is not driven by what ey is like - but by what team they were part of. - networking is critical. if you are the kind of person who waits for work to be assigned to them - you are not going to progress. ey is the place that expects you to hold your career in your own hands, and take initiative to network and find work to do. without networking, you will not progress at all. overall, i would recommend working here if you are ambitious, hard working, and have goals you want to achieve.

productive and growth. of course! here s a longer, well - balanced version that emphasizes ey s positive culture while acknowledging the demanding nature of audit work : - - - * * draft paragraph : * * working at ey has been an incredibly rewarding experience. the firm fosters a genuinely healthy and supportive work environment where people are encouraged to grow both professionally and personally. while the nature of audit can be quite hectic at times with tight deadlines, complex engagements, and periods of high pressure the learning opportunities are immense. every challenge becomes a chance to develop new skills, strengthen technical knowledge, and enhance teamwork and leadership abilities. what truly stands out at ey is the collaborative culture and the emphasis on well - being, mentorship, and continuous development. the organization ensures that even in a fast - paced environment, there is always guidance, recognition, and encouragement to keep moving forward. overall, ey provides not just a workplace, but a community that helps its people learn.

great learning environment, fast pace, and strong name recognition. working at ey as a staff accountant was both challenging and rewarding. the firm offers an incredible amount of exposure to different industries, clients, and technical areas, which has accelerated my learning far faster than i expected. the training resources are extensive, and there s always someone knowledgeable to reach out to when you need help. ey s brand name also opens a lot of doors professionally. the culture is generally supportive, and most teams genuinely want you to succeed. expectations are high, but so is the level of collaboration. i ve had great experiences with senior staff and managers who take the time to coach and provide clear feedback. that said, the workload can be intense, especially during busy seasons. long hours are the norm, and balancing personal commitments can be difficult from january to april. at times, staffing can feel stretched, and it s easy to feel overwhelmed with competing deadlines. while leadership is working on improving work life balance, it s still an area that needs progress.

good workplace. my review of working at ey working at ey has been a valuable experience overall, especially early in my career. the firm offers strong training, structured career development, and a genuinely supportive culture. i felt that people were approachable, and there was a clear emphasis on helping juniors grow quickly. workload & expectations the workload can fluctuate heavily depending on the project and the season. during busy periods, long hours are common, especially in consulting and transaction - related work. still, ey generally maintains a better work life balance compared to more intense industries like investment banking or pure strategy consulting. outside peak times, the workload learning & career growth one of the biggest advantages of ey is the learning curve. from day one, i had exposure to real clients and meaningful responsibility. the firm invests a lot in training programs, certifications, and structured promotion paths. growth is predictable, although it can feel bureaucratic at times. culture i genuinely appreciated the culture at ey. the environment is team - oriented and relatively non - hierarchical for a large professional services firm. people are collaborative, and the atmosphere is more supportive than competitive. of course, experiences vary by team, but overall i felt respected and included. compensation compensation is competitive for the big 4, but it doesn ' t match what you d earn in top - tier consulting or banking. bonuses are modest. however, the benefits, stability, and international mobility opportunities make up for some of that difference. what i liked * great learning environment and steep development curve * supportive and collaborative colleagues * strong brand name that opens doors * clear promotion structure * good global opportunities what could be better * pay could be higher relative to workload * bureaucracy can slow things down * work hours vary greatly by project and season ey is a great place to start or build a career if you want strong professional development, a recognizable brand, and a supportive culture. while the workload can be demanding at times, the overall experience is rewarding and sets you up well for future opportunities in consulting, finance, or corporate roles.

big 4. my experience as a senior auditor at ey was both challenging and highly rewarding. the role provided significant exposure to complex audits across a range of industries, which helped me develop a strong understanding of risk assessment, internal controls, and financial reporting under tight deadlines. ey places a strong emphasis on quality and professional standards, and this was evident in the structured audit methodologies and thorough review processes. as a senior auditor, i had the opportunity to lead audit engagements, manage and mentor junior team members, and communicate directly with clients, which greatly enhanced my leadership and stakeholder management skills. the work environment was fast - paced and demanding, particularly during peak audit seasons, but it encouraged continuous learning and resilience. ey also offers excellent training resources and career development opportunities, allowing employees to build both technical and soft skills. overall, my experience at ey was instrumental in shaping my professional growth and preparing me for more advanced roles in the accounting and finance field.

my sweet experience working at kpmg nigeria. learn everyday. you might get carried away. a typical day at work a typical day at kpmg nigeria is dynamic and fast - paced. it often begins with team check - ins, followed by client meetings, project execution, and collaboration across departments. the environment demands agility, attention to detail, and a strong sense of ownership. no two days are the same, which keeps the work both challenging and stimulating. what i learned working at kpmg has sharpened my strategic thinking, stakeholder management, and problem - solving skills. i ve learned how to navigate complex business environments, deliver value under pressure, and communicate effectively with senior executives. the exposure to diverse industries and high - impact projects has been instrumental in my professional growth. management management at kpmg nigeria is structured and performance - driven. leaders are accessible and supportive, with a strong emphasis on mentorship and accountability. while expectations are high, there is also a clear commitment to developing talent and recognizing contributions. workplace culture the culture is collaborative, intellectually stimulating, and rooted in excellence. there s a strong sense of purpose and pride in the work we do. diversity of thought is encouraged, and teamwork is central to how we deliver results. the firm also promotes continuous learning and innovation. the hardest part of the job the most challenging aspect is managing tight deadlines across multiple high - stakes projects. balancing client expectations with internal quality standards requires resilience and strong time management. it s demanding but also rewarding. the most enjoyable part of the job the most enjoyable part is the opportunity to work with brilliant minds on meaningful projects that shape industries and communities. the sense of impact, the camaraderie among colleagues, and the constant learning make the experience deeply fulfilling.
