toxic middle management / no work - life balance. meaningful high - impact work, strong learning environment, opportunity for problem - solving, innovative focus. toxic management, high volatility, corporate jargon - heavy culture, poor work - life balance, impacted confidence. a typical day at work a typical day at novartis involves partnering directly with production sites to tackle some of their most complex operational challenges. this hands - on engagement with real problems is genuinely stimulating and by far the most rewarding part of the role. what i learned the environment exposes you to cutting - edge pharmaceutical operations and fast - evolving business priorities. you learn to navigate high - stakes decision - making, adapt quickly, and develop a strong technical and strategic problem - solving mindset. management middle management is the weakest aspect of the organization. the culture at this level tends to be deeply perfectionistic and highly micromanaged, with behaviors that can feel undermining or even gas - lighting. trust can be difficult to establish, and if you re not careful, the environment can gradually erode your confidence. workplace culture the work culture leans heavily corporate full of buzzwords, overly polished messaging, and powerpoint - driven communication. while some may appreciate the structure, others may find it insincere or overly scripted. additionally, there is effectively no work - life balance ; being reachable on days off, during vacations, and in the evenings is often treated as an expectation rather than an exception. hardest part of the job navigating the toxic elements of middle management, the blurred boundaries between work and personal time, and the volatility that comes with the company s full pivot to innovative medicines can be especially challenging. the pace is intense, and the sense of organizational instability is real you re never more than a couple of bad quarters away from major disruption. most enjoyable part of the job despite the challenges, the opportunity to work directly on meaningful, high - impact problems at production sites is truly fulfilling. solving complex issues that directly influence patient access and product quality is the part of the job that makes the experience worthwhile.

very different to the private sector, inefficient and parental. i wouldn ' t recommend unicef to someone who has been a high performer in the private sector as the culture and priorities are so different and you ' re assessed in a very different way. i found unicef slow and inefficient, with control tightly held by the senior leadership team ( slt ). for example, renewing a contract for an existing service worth under 40k took 4 - months and many hours of effort, including needing to gain the ceo ' s approval at 4 separate points in addition to multiple approvals from other members of the slt. it also put a lot of work on suppliers. i found the line management approach time - intensive and grounded in my manager telling me i needed to be happy ( rather than listening to me ), with forced intimacy and little respect for difference or personal boundaries. our frequent catch ups focused on values, how unicef wanted me to develop as a person, and making sure i was personally okay - i would have preferred a professional focus. the slt talks a lot about being values - led and unicef invests heavily into equality and diversity and actively welcomes people with different characteristics. this is positive and consistent with the many initiatives taken in the private sector ; however, i was surprised by the judgements made on personality traits rather than professional behaviour and the expectation that you are a certain way. rather than any open discussions, it was being treated with hostility rather than the unicef values that made me realise i ' d been judged to not fit the culture before i ' d even had a chance to learn what the culture was. the leadership team seems to be composed of people who have worked in the charity sector throughout their careers and prioritise doing things the same way they ' ve always been done. rather than staff wellbeing simply being important, i felt it was prioritised over the charitable cause. i didn ' t like that there were so many away days including venues and meals and felt i was already heavily spoiled with the short work hours, good salary ( considering the low levels of authority ), and generous time given to non - business activities. as i joined unicef for the charitable cause i felt guilty and unfulfilled that i wasn ' t being pushed to help improve performance, and that i was actively hindered to do my day - to - day job.

fast paced and meaningful impact. real patient impact, collaboration, culture, growth opportunities, competitive compensation, summer fridays. some unrealistic plans. i started at poseida right out of college and, to be honest, thought that this would be a stepping stone for me to get experience and move on to another company - that was almost a decade ago. there have been a lot of changes within poseida during this time, but three things have always been true : 1 ) we have great technology, 2 ) we have great people, and 3 ) we care about patients. i think poseida does great at providing opportunities for employees to grow into areas that they ' re interested in, even if it ' s not part of their day - to - day obligations, and there are so many people that are excited to share their knowledge. as with any place, there are challenges though, and i think poseida sometimes struggles with wanting to move so fast to get new and exciting therapies to patients in need, that employees have to scramble to meet ambitious timelines. with that being said, it has always been worth it when you see clinical data showing that the therapy you helped bring to the clinic is now giving people time back that they didn ' t have before.

job security is zero. salary. management. working at pfizer offers a fast - paced and dynamic work environment where each day brings new challenges and opportunities to learn. the projects are impactful, and the chance to contribute to life - changing innovations is deeply rewarding. however, the experience is overshadowed by significant concerns about job security. while i gained invaluable skills and grew professionally during my time here, the frequent layoffs despite the company s substantial financial success create a sense of instability and apprehension. it s disheartening to see dedicated, hardworking employees affected by these decisions, which undermines morale and long - term loyalty. management quality varies, with some leaders focusing on employee development and others emphasizing results above all else. the culture is driven by innovation and high performance, which can be both motivating and stressful, depending on the team dynamics and the level of support from leadership. the most challenging part of the job is the uncertainty surrounding continued employment, while the most enjoyable aspect is the opportunity to work alongside talented colleagues on meaningful projects that have a real impact on global health. overall, pfizer is an excellent place to grow and contribute to important work, but the lack of job security is a significant drawback that potential employees should consider.

too much layoff. this pharmaceutical company has developed a poor reputation due to its frequent layoffs, which have significantly impacted employee morale and stability. high turnover rates have created an atmosphere of uncertainty, making it difficult for teams to maintain momentum or long - term projects. many skilled professionals have left, leading to a loss of institutional knowledge and inconsistent workflow. the constant restructuring disrupts communication between departments and affects productivity, while remaining employees face increased workloads and pressure. public perception has also suffered, as layoffs often raise concerns about the company s financial health and leadership decisions. potential applicants may hesitate to join, fearing job insecurity. although the organization may still produce quality products, its inability to maintain a stable workforce undermines overall performance. for sustainable success, management must address the root causes of downsizing and prioritize a culture of retention, support, and transparent communication to rebuild trust with both employees and stakeholders.

revolving door environment. decent benefits, historical reputation, transparent salary bands. unpaid overtime, high turnover, understaffing, favouritism and backstabbing, lack of support and empathy. pros : decent benefits : manulife offers good benefits to its permanent employees. historical reputation : previously known as a great place to work, indicating potential for improvement. transparent salary bands : salary bands are transparent up to the director level, providing clarity on compensation. cons : constant overtime : salaried employees are expected to work 45 - 60 hours per week without overtime pay. high turnover : significant turnover rate, making it difficult to retain high - quality staff. understaffing : teams are kept understaffed, leading to high stress and burnout. favouritism and backstabbing : vps, avps and directors show favouritism, leading to a toxic work environment. lack of support and empathy : the work environment is high - stress, cut - throat, and lacks support. outdated offices : office environments are outdated, contributing to a less appealing work atmosphere. avoidance of negative news : vps only handle good news, burying negative information, leading to misinformed decision - making.

a great place to work, good pay, but a russian roulette for culture. higher pay, than other local employers. too much admin - related monitoring. mandatory rto, pmt, timesheets, planview. low manpower resulting to heavy workload. as a senior business analyst for the treasury team of manulife business processing services ( " mbps " ), we were assigned to support and philippine - based projects during our day - to - day. we were either technical supports - supporting the coding, and creation of excel / macro - based mappers or other apps ; business analysts - writing the brd and running projects ; change management people - supporting change management efforts, such as hypercare, trainings, and communications of projects. during my time there, i learned to be intentional on my efforts and on how i spend my time so that there will be little to no missed items on my plate. mbps ' s management is so - so. locally, the director and team leads of our department were fun and supportive, however, some of the other leads in other departments were not. i also noticed that there is a great emphasis put on the hierarchy among the higher leaders, with lower - level leaders always walking on eggshells around those higher than them. it was kind of frustrating as it led to some directives that were technically micromanagement. for the culture, mbps has a mixed one. for our team, i observed that it was a culture of belongingness and collective excellence. the leaders supported employee growth and expanding their knowledge base. the leaders are also supportive of any learning and development efforts undertaken by the staffs. on the other hand, our team ' s leaders are also fun to hang out with outside work, often organizing jogs, sporting events, online game tournament, off - work night outs, and vacation trips, which were the best parts of my stay there. the hardest part of the job was the workload, especially at the start of 2025, where multiple projects were dumped on our team without clear discussions and introductions whatsoever. while, yes, they could be considered as challenges and opportunities to grow, too much challenge can burn out employees. i ' ve observed that most of us in our team were demotivated and burnt out during my last 6 months with the company. the most enjoyable part of the job is getting to work on exciting projects for the department. i really enjoy designing new processes, even though i am not a technical person, but designing process

smart people, but less stable than you ' d think. the technology staff is very smart and the chief data office is well run and fairly visionary. the pay scale in technology is pretty good for managers at the vp level and above but has a steep drop - off for less senior staff despite the " lead " and " director " titles. work - life balance is technically good but a lot of the days are full of meetings - - the company knows this and constantly discusses how to make things less meeting - oriented, but it ' s ingrained in the culture. as a consequence, after - hours is the only time a lot of people are able to get work done. they ' ve been going all - in on agile methodologies, which i think makes for a good experience for people who want to learn more about project planning and delivery. you really do learn to plan things on a fine - grain level every quarter and schedule needs with other groups ahead of time, and this was a good experience. however, there ' s a silent boom / bust cycle that goes on behind the scenes where the company may scale up an initiative and then suddenly shrink it down. if you ' re in infrastructure or working in their core lines of business, you ' re probably more insulated from those issues, but any new initiatives and lines of business are inherently risky. to support this scale up / scale down culture, they also have a lot of contractors you will end up working with.

good. a typical day at work : a typical day for an office boy in banking might involve assisting with office tasks like filing, delivering documents, serving tea / coffee, and maintaining office cleanliness. interactions with colleagues and sometimes clients are also part of the day. what you believe you learned : in this role, you likely learned about office procedures, importance of attention to detail, and how to interact professionally in a banking environment. you might have gained skills in time management and adaptability to different tasks. management : management in a banking environment typically involves overseeing operations to ensure efficiency, compliance with banking regulations, and a professional work environment. as an office boy, you ' d likely report to a senior staff member or administration. workplace culture : workplace culture in banking tends to be professional, with emphasis on security, accuracy, and customer service. teamwork and discretion with sensitive information are likely valued. the hardest part of the job : the hardest part might be managing multiple tasks efficiently while maintaining attention to detail, dealing with varying priorities from different colleagues, or handling tasks that require precision in a fast - paced environment. the most enjoyable part of the job : the most enjoyable part might be interacting with colleagues, learning about different aspects of banking operations, and contributing to the smooth functioning of the office. helping others and being part of a team could also be fulfilling.

definitely not fraud investigation. hybrid position, benefits, pay. management, systems, hybrid position, mentality. starting with training, they do not cover what is needed for the position and gloss over the important parts of the position. it ' s constantly hammered into you that you ' re part of the citi family, which is a huge red flag. being told by managers that they supposedly understand the stressors of the position even that they too wanted to throw their equipment out a window when in this position. real encouraging. also that it ' s told to analysts that most don ' t last a year within a call center environment or the department for that matter. the system you ' re forced to work with constantly has issues along with the equipment they provide even citing that you ' ll probably need to replace within the first year of your working with and it ' s a nightmare to do so. combating with off - shore agents for both escalations and base analyst work. metrics are always a key issue when it comes to feedback which is discouraging. you ' re expected to be a customer service agent rather than a fraud analyst, even having metrics for customer satisfaction that should not be part of fraud. if one metric is out of line, it will hold you back from advancing. you get the brunt of issues that stem from other departments and get yelled at constantly by clients. there ' s rarely downtime between calls and it ' s an expectation you do work between calls only to get interrupted. management seems to be away from their computer constantly taking far too long to get resolution or just not online entirely. the only things that were enjoyed was the ability to work from home, but even that was stressful. pay is decent for the position and benefits are as well.

review of citibank citi mortgage. diverse and inclusive culture, supportive team environment, exposure to complex loan products ; rewarding to help clients achieve homeownership. high workload during peak periods, inconsistent communication across teams, strong pressure to meet targets, job security impacted by market shifts. typical day at work : fast - paced and detail - oriented, with a strong focus on underwriting accuracy, compliance, and meeting turn times. days often involved collaborating with processors, loan officers, and management to resolve situations that may arise with complex files. my team specialized in manual underwriting and affordable housing programs, so problem - solving and guideline interpretation were part of the daily routine. what i learned : i built a deep understanding of conventional and non - conforming loan products, as well as affordable housing guidelines. i strengthened my ability to analyze layered risk factors, apply regulatory frameworks, and maintain compliance under strict audit standards. beyond technical skills, i honed client - facing communication, time management, and the ability to work under sustained pressure. management : management was supportive and knowledgeable, particularly within my specialized team. at times, communication across different groups was uneven, and leadership styles varied depending on the department. that said, my immediate managers encouraged professional growth and provided guidance when tackling challenging files. workplace culture : citibank promoted diversity and inclusivity, and overall, the culture supported a healthy work - life balance. however, workload spikes especially during market surges could be stressful, with long days to meet performance goals and closing deadlines. hardest part of the job : the high expectations for accuracy and speed were demanding, particularly with difficult or time - sensitive loan scenarios. balancing strict investor / agency guidelines with the need to serve clients quickly required focus and resilience. most enjoyable part of the job : helping families achieve homeownership especially through affordable housing programs was the most rewarding part of the role. closing a complex loan after working through obstacles brought a real sense of accomplishment and pride in the work. note on departure : citibank underwent major restructuring that eliminated thousands of positions across the firm, including mine. while it was challenging to leave, i valued my time there and the expertise i gained.

well rounded company with opportunity for growth. diversity, opportunity for career growth. global teams bring global hours. make sure you have the support on the team that you need. i feel that my review of citi would be rather bitter after 24 years of service and suddenly being laid off without warning ( so i am not sure what that really says about job security ), but other than that, an honest review... citi does allow for growth within the company if you are able to put in the time and effort to do so. i worked on the technology side and that team showed concern for its employees at least at the team level. work / life balance is as available there as it is at other large corporations and come with the same planning that your work day / week / month brings. as with anything at citi, it is available if you are able to plan for it and have the time to do so with a busy workload. this includes training, advancement, work / life, and flexibility. i learned that you should not be too modest.... if you have an accomplishment, make sure it is known to your management and that you keep track of it for any review you may have. i learned also that you are only as valuable as your last accomplishment. but i would guess that is the same at many large corporations now. on a more positive side, i loved the people that i worked with and met over the years, and citi certainly embraces diversity, which i loved to see. while i have heard there were issues with management amongst other team / groups, i was lucky enough to work with amazing managers, leadership, and mentors throughout my career here. given the opportunity, i would work there again to potentially bring a management style that embraces team and individual accomplishments with even more visibility.

protecting people and their assets. cultural events, diversity, benefits and options to work from home. management stretched too far, inadequate online, work from home training. typically, i would start my day by signing in online, opening all required job assist tools, signing in to the phone system, and checking which direction i would be taking for any given day. i learned how to navigate various systems required for looking for fraud trends. i believe immediate management was stretched too far. during my tenure, i noticed managers going from being responsible for a team of 10 workers to over twice that amount, making it far too difficult for them to remain team member - focused. i lost my position with the bank because i was one of over 22 people on a single team, and i could not get the side - by - sides that i felt i needed to improve. i cannot blame my last manager for that because his focus was on over 22 people as opposed to 10 - 15, and unlike prior years, i wasn ' t able to get the support that i needed. otherwise, the culture at the company was fabulous. there are many cultural events held throughout the year to help with overall company morale, one of my favorites being " snow in the citi, " which is done every year at my local citibank campus. the most difficult part of my last position was understanding some of the patterns in the tools used to detect fraud trends. i got very little support in learning them better in order to make my reviews flow more easily, as well as faster. that was very disappointing. i loved being able to work from home and the occasional meeting in the office for camaraderie with the team. i really enjoyed the benefits that the company offered, keeping myself and my family healthy, along with the perks of being able to purchase through purchasing power, making necessary home items affordable for me.

there is none. disinformation, dishonest promotion, lack of training, and insufficient managerial support. a typical day at work : i process assigned tasks and coordinate with my manager regarding incoming tasks and current issues. what i believe i learned : i have learned a lot on my own ; however, i was never provided with full training for the tasks assigned to me. i was always completed my task. management : having been with the company for five years and experiencing seven managers in the same team, i can say that only one manager truly understands and cares for the team. workplace culture : the workplace culture is lacking. it often feels like it s about who you know. most higher - level managers are overseas, which makes it difficult to understand and collaborate effectively. they are not familiar with american values and legal processes. the hardest part of the job : collaborating with managers who are overseas or from different countries can be challenging, as they often do not understand american laws and legal requirements. the most enjoyable part of the job : i enjoy completing all the tasks assigned to me, even though i know that managers tend to promote individuals they are associated with.

excellent exposure to risk & governance, demanding workload. citi exposes you to complex, enterprise level work. you gain deep experience in governance, regulatory expectations, operational risk, and cross functional collaboration skills that translate anywhere. leadership quality varies by team. some managers are excellent at communication and alignment, while others struggle with clarity, workload balancing, or stakeholder engagement. a typical day at citi involved managing complex change initiatives, coordinating with cross functional teams, and ensuring risks were clearly identified and addressed early. i learned how to navigate large scale, highly regulated environments and strengthened my skills in stakeholder engagement, governance, and operational discipline. management varied by team, but strong leaders emphasized transparency, structure, and collaboration. the workplace culture was fast paced and demanding, with a strong focus on controls, documentation, and meeting regulatory expectations. the hardest part of the job was balancing competing priorities across multiple workstreams while maintaining high - quality execution. the most enjoyable part was partnering with talented colleagues, solving meaningful problems, and contributing to improvements that had real impact across the organization.

this is the only company i know that have 4 - 5 reorganizations a year and still don ' t have a good plan in place. good benefits and decent pay. no stability or security, no work - life balance, no morale, no structure. leadership changes responsibilities daily and moves resources around to put them in places that make no sense to their skillset. for this to be an agile environment, there are a million useless or redundant meetings daily. leadership does not plan well, and work - life balance is horrible. employee morale is minus zero, and no one cares. they do surveys yearly, but never address the real issues. there is absolutely zero job security here, and reorganizations are done on a quarterly basis in many of the areas i have worked in. the hardest part of my job is figuring out what my job description / responsibilities are this week, since they change so frequently. employees are not valued.

a fast - paced and dynamic work environment with valuable learning opportunities, but with challenges that come with being a contractor. what is the best part of working at the company? the best part of working at santander as a contractor is the professional work environment and the opportunity to collaborate with a skilled team. the company provides access to valuable resources and learning experiences, allowing me to develop my technical skills while contributing to meaningful projects. what is the most stressful part about working at the company? as a contractor at santander, the most stressful part is the uncertainty of contract extensions and job stability. additionally, adapting to changing priorities and meeting tight deadlines can be challenging, but it also helps improve problem - solving skills and resilience. what is the work environment and culture like at the company? the work environment at santander is professional and fast - paced, with a strong focus on collaboration and customer service. the company promotes a culture of teamwork, learning, and adaptability. while expectations are high, the support from colleagues and leadership helps create a positive and productive atmosphere. what is a typical day like for you at the company? a typical day at santander as a contractor involves troubleshooting technical issues, supporting end - users with hardware and software problems, managing tickets, and ensuring smooth it operations. communication and collaboration are key, as i often work with different teams to resolve issues efficiently. the fast - paced environment keeps things dynamic, and every day brings new challenges and learning opportunities.

great environment with transparency and empathy. what is the best part of working at the company? the team and management were excellent. what is the most stressful part about working at the company? the commute over the washington bridge. what is the work environment and culture like at the company? it was a very positive and supportive work environment. they encouraged employee collaboration. what is a typical day like for you at the company? review files and make decisions based on company manuals. open communication on teams with colleagues created an environment where everyone would openly share their ideas and actively listening to different circumstances during meetings.

terrible company to work for. none. low pay, unethical behaviors, uncommon sales pressure, poor management. zero culture opportunities unless you want to drive 2 hours to nyc. terrible branch management. managers are aloud to do / go / run branches however they wish with no repercussion. multiple times a week i am pressured to " ask for favors " from businesses, open unneeded products for customers, turn away customers who are not affluent or not white. i have experience at 5 different banks. in my opinion santander is worse than all of them including wells fargo. terrible company to work for.
good stable workplace. good benefits. good management. i enjoy working here. the pay is pretty normal for this type of job. you get bonuses as well which is a good plus. overall solid place to work.

stressful and busy with little or no assistance. long hours. the whole job was remote, with no in person demonstration of any equipment or programme use. i was very unprepared and had to re - do training because i was not shown how to process calls

ok. good pay, however expectations of advisors are too high, am too many processes that are changing all the time, slightly micro managed, after finishing a call if you re not back on another call within 5 mins expect a message

good pay and benefits but unpleasant environment. ceo great, friendly and approachable. open to feedback. a lot of senior management unapproachable and middle management toxic. good pay and benefits. too much red tape.

very toxic, both literally and figuratively. what is the best part of working at the company? sociable working hours, no late nights or sundays what is the most stressful part about working at the company? terrible management, non supportive, neglectful, dishonest, unsafe working environment. what is the work environment and culture like at the company? everyone hates working there, no one ever regrets leaving. best thing i ever did. what is a typical day like for you at the company? serving customers, fixing atms that break constantly. being spoken to like dirt by management & customers.

glad to leave. management don t communicate with each other. difficult to contact hr a lot of pressure to reach targets and not much consideration for customers. feeling like you have to rush through calls when some customers need more time.

poor toxic culture. senior leaders and managers do not support lower bands and protect each other. toxic environment with s2s overworked rather than s3 / 4 s. encouraged to speak up however they promote a culture of bullying to frighten colleagues if they do by pushing them out the door. over worked staff and low salaries.

least bad call centre ive worked at. when i was there it was professional and i felt supported, granted i was lucky with managers but when my health took a bad turn i wasnt just kicked the curb. alot of students so fairly high turn over but nothing compared to others

workload doesn ' t match pay. countless unnecessary tasks are expected to be done on top of the most important - helping customers, but the salary doesn ' t reflect this. some team managers are also very micromanaging and nitpicky.

it was an experience to say the least. what is the best part of working at the company? the best part was the people you meet what is the most stressful part about working at the company? when you try apply for a position that you qualify for and they give it to someone that don ' t know anything about what you ' re doing what is the work environment and culture like at the company? it ' s ok not too bad and not that stressful what is a typical day like for you at the company? very busy and always on the go
decent workplace. large company, mgmt changes often, fair pay, ability to learn and grow, good benefits, training offered, travel sometimes required, college degree needed for some positions

not worth it. what is the best part of working at the company? you can hear the entertainment while working. what is the most stressful part about working at the company? harassment. hours are not secure. unprofessional management. what is the work environment and culture like at the company? it ' s like highschool. rumors and gossip. the men harass the women. what is a typical day like for you at the company? cleaning before event, during and after. restrooms, halls and seating area.

typical banking role. cut throat culture. miss your target and you re out. well paid you re only as good as the team around you, expect plenty of training a nurturing needed to help you speak to people.

stressful and frustrating. what is the best part of working at the company? hours are good. employee benefits are good if you are into tech stuff. these are the only best parts of working for this company what is the most stressful part about working at the company? management don t care. you are expected to adjust to different manager styles on a regular basis. very cliquey crowd and if you don t fit the mould, you will be overlooked for promotion. salary is poor and expected to work overtime with very little notice what is the work environment and culture like at the company? work environment is unhealthy. most people tend to put on weight. management get a lot more benefits which they tend to brag about to the lower staff. unhelpful and generally stressful environment what is a typical day like for you at the company? a typical day would be logging on and making myself available for calls at start time. investigating issues and listening to previous calls also takes up part of the day. targets are to close as many complaints as possible with reasonable outcome. careful of other staff members taking credit for your work as they are under pressure to make their case closures add up

not the worst. it s a good role for a good company. staff are treated well although from time to time the workplace can be very stressful with needing to cover customers face to face and over the phone. plenty of benefits within the company.

micromanagement. micro - management is the standard. constant calls from your manager under the guise of checking if you need anything, but really checking up. whilst in the office don ' t even think of talking - even though you are there for ' collaboration '. all work is timed and if you have anything remotely complex that takes longer than the expected 6 minutes be prepared to explain yourself as if you have broken some law. the company bang on about mental wellbeing but treat staff actually don ' t care. there is zero flexibility, request to alter your hours is a flat out no.

nice place to work, good atmosphere and well supported by managers. good flexibility to work from home. none. good workplace culture with plenty going on to keep you busy. management is generally good. typical day is answering calls and resolving customer issues, can be stressful during busy periods but on the whole it is okay

fast paced environemnt. santander is a great place to work for but you must be ready for a fast paced environment with things changing on a regular basis. flexibility is there for you as long as this meets business demand and you will have a great relationship with your manager who is open to discuss things when needed.
stressful. very stressful atmosphere created by the higher management. they do not care one iota about their staff. micromanagement has taken over and it is not a pleasant place to work anymore.

ultra stressful and draining. if you ' re looking to sacrifice your mental stability just to get the bills paid, this company is the place for you to be. micromanagement, no care for your wellbeing, bullying, unrealistic targets with toxic management, zero support and shrinking resources whilst expectations getting higher. you ' re never good enough for what you do, constantly getting yelled.
under the cosh. mentors on the job role are great if they are ever available. case reviews can drag into the weeks. management constantly on your back. glad to have got out of there.

if you have a good professional branch manager, your job will be great. when everyone is happy and everything is done exactly in balance. when unprofessional, unempathetic managers appear and destroy the atmosphere and system of work in the branch. i work face to face with clients every day. i conduct communication, listen to clients ' needs. learned to work with difficult clients and stressful situations. it ' s nice when a customer leaves in a good mood and thanks for the help.
good benefits. great benefits. if you re looking for a job where you can grow this is the place. the only downside is the pay is average. not good but not terrible.

productive and customer satisfaction. work life balance. adherence to processes which can seem like a maze. typical day : a typical day is the back to back calls, card compromise resolution. transaction reviews and fraud investigations and reporting. raising disputes with merchants and customer feedback regarding disputes. what i learned : i learned how to navigate the systems for call resolution. management : support from managers is readily available. work place culture : interesting and great people to work with. teamwork and collaboration is essential. hardest part of the job : the occasional irate customer. customer dissatisfaction reporting. enjoyable part of the job : every call is different and can be interesting and enlightening. great conversations.

advancement opportunities. the training was in depth. the calls were hectic and never ending. there was plenty of opportunities for advancement, schedule was decent and plenty of overtime opportunities as well. work life balance was non existent. pay was decent.

very stressful job, back to back calls, expected to start before time and work beyond office hrs. no over time given. they keep raising the bar but don t do the same to the salary. no flexible time, no over times. they extract the maximum out of you till you give up and quit. exploitive culture, everything is micro managed. people are quitting left, right and centre so more work load for the people who are trying to hang in there. company doesn t care for about both the customers and its employees exploring both in the best possible way. all they care about is their profits

overworked and too high of standards. standards are way too high, everything is critiqued in the job. it s a basic call centre job but being on a call too long is bad for your overall performance, taking shortcuts is bad, managers are useless and just constantly on breaks to go smoke or eat. no incentives and worst pay

best place to work. work life balance. could be stressful at times. love my job, gives me a sense of purpose. inclusivity is amazing everyone is welcome here. the work life balance is great, as long as you do your job they work with you about your personal situations.
great place. easy job, great staff! it s a remote environment so you need to be self efficient. but a very easy gig! worked in funding which a glorified auditor position - super easy.

shambles. they do not care about staff loyalty, they would rather outsource work instead. after taking the decision to close the newcastle site they have then removed all progression opportunities from staff based here and are slowly getting rid of us.
not great. no progression opportunities. lure you in with a good salary and then offshore your teams. lack of interaction with senior leaders. peakon survey feedback never listened to.

not too bad. worked here for a long time and wasn ' t the worst place i ' ve worked. some of the attitudes of staff were awful and the overall company just sees a number not a person but i was lucky to work with a good group
good. the job is fantastic management good and bonus scheme as well as pay progression is amazing. good recognition given also by the company when achieving goals

good benefits. you will be very busy, a lot of calls, good schedule and advancement opportunities. the downside was dealing with other employees in the csr and management was questionable including the centers manager. training was great and in depth.

good company but short staffed. management was very knowledgeable. days were long and always understaffed. pay was fair at best for the amount of loans we closed. it s too bad the company dissolved the department and over 100 of us lost our jobs.

terrible management. pto. it sucks and management is not polite. more tasks were often delegated to those whom had hard work ethic. same pay as others but more work. financial reporting management.. had a lack of communication with other management. often i felt i was the messenger between managers rather than them actually speaking to each other. i was need up leaving on my own and have had better luck elsewhere.

don t work here. colleagues. management. corp & comm management are clueless with constant changes & restructuring. ceo said under performance is not tolerated in an all staff email. you attend conferences about growth plans then make staff redundant. they ask to open accounts as part of targets but then decide to close down the account opening teams for a month before putting in a replacement. you need lending but credit team don t sign off any deals. you need deposits but rates are bottom of market. online banking is awful and breaks down every week.

overworked like a dog and you do two jobs. be prepared to be overworked like a dog : you do two jobs you work in the branch and in the call centre, you are made to run and jump between workstations and they don t like you to waste any second, you close the branch and they don t let you breath, they demand you immediately take calls even if there are customers left at the branch, you are like a dog and horse jumping across channels and doing different jobs without the chance to catch your breath. they micromanage you by the second. they make you work like a dog. it does not feel like working in financial services, it is more like working in the hospitality sector, and they have a whole nonsense of being hospitality ready. branch employees should not be call handlers they have so many security and aml things to think about. i now work for another bank where i do not have to take calls and i am so much more happy and they don t micromanage me and put you call centre targets and branch targets when you work on a branch, you just need to do your branch job.

team are great but workplace is very pressured. fast moving, pressured environment. i accepted this customer service role and then discovered the sales kpi s i was supposed to deliver. training is sporadic and piecemeal but you are expected to deliver over and above at all points. for the just above minimum wage salary this is a big ask. if you are passionate about banking and have a lot of determination this may be for you, for everyone else it probably isn t.

problematic. what is the best part of working at the company? pay was good but detrimental to mental health. unreliable managers and ever changing computer systems. wouldnt reccomend what is the most stressful part about working at the company? not being able to solve customer queries due to decisions being made by different departments that had nothing to do with being a fraud adviser and having to deal with angry customers. the impact on my mental health of the overall job was not worth it but pay was good what is the work environment and culture like at the company? remote so cant say much to that

excellent culture. wonderful company, excellent culture. the employers really care about their employees. i would recommend this place of work to anyone that asked me. i have been here almost 15 years!

it was awesome until they decided to go overseas with jobs. it is a good company.. for executives lots of layoffs not sure if they are even in florida now used to pay bonus. got a new ceo with no good value for the phone related employees
fun place great employer. great company cares about employees. has special events great pto plan lots of benefits managers work hard and care only issue is they closed the colorado office
good intro to retail banking. big company, possible to get solid exposure amongst different lines of business. pay is on low side. bigger internationally and often felt limited in us market
good colleagues. okay place to work, wonderful co workers that are always willing to help. hours are reasonable, just don t take work home with you and you will be fine.

great culture! working at santander was a rewarding and valuable experience. i had the opportunity to strengthen my skills in fraud prevention, customer service, and transaction analysis. i was part of a professional and dynamic team focused on delivering fast and secure solutions. i m grateful for the growth opportunities and continuous learning i experienced during my time there.

fun workplace if you re in the right team. the company itself is decent to work for but genuinely the people you work with are what make the job. worked for santander for a few years and the hybrid approach to work was ideal for me. only thing to note is some of the managers tend to micromanage..

every day is different. lots of changes happening, a lot of micromanagement every minute of the day is tracked which is tricky when you are customer facing and have different issues arising every day. systems are old and dated needs a lot of investment to bring it up to date, poor training for staff but branch teams are fantastic

worst job i ve had. good benefits, pension & bonus. overworked & underpaid. to put it simply it s very micromanaged and a toxic environment. you are paid just above minimum wage to basically be a sales person and if you don t hit unrealistic targets you will hear about it. new hires like myself who weren t even trained to a high enough standard leads to you feeling stressed and like anything you do isn t good enough. truly takes a toll on your mental health. another issue is the way they go on about the call centre stuff. certain collegues don t have to do any calls but seems like the newer ones get stuck on them for hours at a time. they push so hard for people to be on the phone it means things in the branch aren t being done well enough. management is shocking and with improvement maybe the place will be a nicer space to work in. truly you are just a number there and it doesn t matter if you burn out you just have to go above and beyond for them.

don ' t do it. you will be trapped, but used and unappreciated, they don ' t appreciate long service and knowledge, like to restrict where you can go in the company unless you are the favourite and management think they are better than anyone else

good agents / horrible management. the upper management does not promote within. they don ' t appreciate you at all. the vp ( who was hired on the outside ) there has a personal vendettas to anyone that is remotely smarter then he is and takes it out on the manager of the team he manages, which makes their job harder. this department needs new upper management immediately.

poor decisions ruined good foothold. insurance, teamwork, work / life balance. dogma in the wrong places. 2 of the 3 managers i worked under were amazing and i loved my time with them. the 3rd and final one was awful. they forged timecards to avoid paying out overtime and hr sided with them on everything. 3rd manager also heavily forced sales and canceled approved pto last minute so they could have off instead. management rating is reflecting of the 3rd only. my first manager was a 4 star and my 2nd a 5. i only give the 3rd a 1 because 0 isn ' t an option.
ok place to work. ok place to work until the merge happened, layoffs happened. after that the environment changed and it wasn t what it once was, which is an absolute shame

great boss. i am especially grateful for my boss their leadership style is both inspiring and approachable, creating a perfect balance of guidance and autonomy. they consistently recognize and celebrate achievements, encourage open communication, and provide constructive feedback that helps me grow. their dedication to the team s success and well - being is unmatched, and i feel fortunate to work under such exceptional leadership.

ok place to step your foot into deparment. fast paced role with fairly bad training as you step into the role not really equipped with dealing with most of the cases to begin with. this is a telephony role with all normal pros and comms that come with it. hard to move up, unless someone leaves. colleagues are nice though.

fulfilling until restructure in 2023. wage & benefits are perks! what is the best part of working at the company? majority of the customers are lovely, my branch had the most incredible team, who supported one another through work issues, personal. what is the most stressful part about working at the company? being treated as a number by senior staff, being micromanaged. what is the work environment and culture like at the company? work hard and get rewarded, however goal posts can be moved. but the annual rewards are worthwhile. what is a typical day like for you at the company? call centre support, face to face, counter service, fixing atms.

used to be a great place to work. unfortunately since covid, there have been many changes, down falls, redundancies & fear whether your job is safe. i worked there for over 12 years, 7 of which were great. with the way of the world & ai taking over everything, certain jobs are now becoming digital or going offshore to other parts of the world.

stressful. micromanagement of every minute, big brother attitude from managers. constantly taking calls of upset customers who yell at you, expect to take 60 calls a day with 70 % annoyed at the tech that santander make them use that s not efficient and guess what, you have to apologise and deal with it like it s your fault, and when serving the customers you are also having problems with the it

just been made redundant. it offers a great work / life balance and support. the pay is aligned with similar roles and fair. there are challenges like most jobs but i did feel very supported in my last two years working there.

i loved being a banker just not at santander. what is the best part of working at the company? absolutely nothing. the management has no care for the employees. what is the most stressful part about working at the company? the only take away from me about being the best place to work was the friends that i made what is the work environment and culture like at the company? very stressful always under pressure never told that you ve done something well but it s pointed out when you don t what is a typical day like for you at the company? lots and lots of paperwork. so much so i feel like i ve killed an entire forest.

fun. great experience, if you re are on call. 0 hour contract no work very often. good as a second or third job but not if its your main job, i waited for a long time for a part time position, years..! then left as it was not giving me any hours at times

challenging work. work is challenging yet rewarding, offering continuous opportunities to grow, adapt, and learn new skills every day. an environment that encouraged adaptability, problem - solving, and collaboration, offering valuable opportunities for career growth.

what do you expect, you ' re just a number. it ' s redundancy central at this place at the moment, the goal is to get rid of everyone, because their loyal staff were finally earning a great wage due to the pay progression policy ( which has not been scrapped ), and hire offshore to save money.
amazing workplace. great work environment, the team was amazing. great communication, and the manager was flexible and understanding. i loved working there, too bad this location closed.
kpi layoffs. all they care about is their kpis, they have been firing a lot of people as a result of that. management not helpful and technology is very behind other big banks. a lot of manual work.

the most enjoyable part of the job. the most enjoyable part of working for santander is the opportunity to make a real difference for customers while being part of a supportive and collaborative team. i enjoy helping customers with their financial needs, whether that s solving a problem, improving their banking experience, or guiding them towards the right products. santander s focus on innovation and customer service also makes the role rewarding, as i feel i m contributing to a business that values both people and progress.

cool work environment, depends on management. i enjoyed my time working as a teller at santander. we had regulars come in weekly, and there weren t many surprises. got a little stressful when management changed, but it still wasn t a terrible place to work.
not bad job. best part about this job is that is remote and you barely come into the office. worst part about the job is the repetitiveness constantly taking call after call is really draining.
fun environment / good management. all of the people were great. it can prettt busy at the branch i was working at but other than that it was great. expect a lot non - english speakers client.

awful. good benefits. everything else. chaotic work environment no appreciation never satisfied no management of staff branches completely disconnected issues are not dealt with professionally avoidance of addressing issues no longer putting the customer first

okay. pros : * really do feel the difference you can make disrupting customer scams. * managers are supportive * colleagues generally are great to work with * shifts for the most part are decent. cons : * the work can be highly stressful, especially when you are trying to convince someone they are being scammed. * customers with blocked accounts tend to be very irate, and it can lead to some very difficult conversations. * pay definitely could be better. * sometimes there really isn t clear guidance on how to tackle a particular process, and can lead to some uncertainty. * the company really are driving towards outsourcing which causes anxiety around job security.

terrible to work there. i found remote work difficult at times because management didn t offer much guidance or communication about expectations. without clear policies or feedback, i often felt lost and uncertain about whether i was doing a good job. i think being in the office occasionally could have helped improve teamwork and direction. in my opinion we should have to go into the office.

decent place to work. room for growth and development. strict on targets and little flexibility for work life balance. from experience of bank call centres, santander was the most ethical, efficient and modern. the management and team were supportive and understanding.

good if you ' re local. overly strict with company policies, understandable with regard to some things within the financial sector, but i found it to be too strict with minor things which gave very little breathing room when it came to travelling miles, such as the penalties for being late were ridiculous when you ' re reliant on unreliable public transportation, company has a lot to learn when it comes to the real lifes of its employees.
not a fun place. high turnover employees know more than management does. i wouldn t go back her very bad environment. then benefits are good that s about it. a lot of layoffs

a very organized and supportive team. what is the best part of working at the company? very flexible and supportive. what is the most stressful part about working at the company? can t think of a thing. there really nothing stands out. worked during covid - had no problems working at home.
bonus. very cliquey so promotions are often based on who you know, not what you know. the bonuses are good and you can work from home some days of the week.
hard work but rewarding. decent company and good benefits. still all about productivity and what you can get out of colleagues. but longevity of role has been key since covid.

fun workplace. working at the santander call centre has been a positive and rewarding experience overall. the training is thorough and genuinely prepares you for handling customer queries with confidence. support from team leaders is consistent, and there s always someone available to help if you re unsure about a process or need guidance on a difficult call. the workplace culture is friendly, and colleagues are quick to offer advice or share tips, which makes the environment feel collaborative rather than competitive. the performance expectations are clear, and feedback is constructive, helping you improve without feeling pressured. while call volumes can get high and some days are more stressful than others, the breaks, support systems, and strong team communication help balance it out. the benefits and job stability are solid, making it a good place for anyone seeking customer service experience in a professional, structured setting.

it s ok ish. pay, progression, flexibility. it s ok you have no targets but you have an expectation to meet, you don t get thanked for what you do, if you want flexibility good luck.. not amazing pay for a bank worker

good company. the bank is good to work for. takes care of their employees. the benefits, pay, time off are great. the work itself is boring and the customers are high stress due to it being their money.

challenging. what is the best part of working at the company? the benifits, everything else was a real challenge. what is the most stressful part about working at the company? the company s expectations, over stretched workloads. not really a friendly place to work, stress levels were always high. what is the work environment and culture like at the company? working environment clean, light, nice, culture aggressive.

peaceful and trouble - free. when i started the role there was an expectation to work hybrid, surprisingly after the initial training there was absolutely no pressure to be in the office. no daily, weekly or monthly check - ins to worry about once you ' re competent in the role, management did not seem to bother you unnecessarily. good tech and systems, the people were friendly enough. peaceful and trouble - free, although this was years ago.

run don ' t bother, because of their system almost became homeless. what is the best part of working at the company? had a great bonus structured and pay. what is the most stressful part about working at the company? working for a team that does not support you. hr uses you as a target to get their investigation done. doesn ' t value as an employee and looks at you as a number. what is the work environment and culture like at the company? toxic upper level are blind to see what is going on. management and hr doesn ' t care about their employees, a lot of favoritism. if you are not part of a click you are screwed. uses therm fairness as a way to get what they want. what is a typical day like for you at the company? no team, running around like your head is being cut off. hr comes around when they need something.

very bad place to work manager are very bad. bad. bad experience. manager are very bad unprofessional very very bad management manager did not do anything they use for other people work for there on benefits i have very bad experience working there they have no experience how to work
demanding and toxic environment. the company had a very demanding and toxic environment, contrary to what they portray themselves as - depending on the department. read the reviews please.

very stable. obviously work place friendliness depends on where you are. but i ' m general a good place to work at. very generous with pto. by the 4 year mark i basically had like 80 + hours of pto i could take and you roll over 40.
fun workplace. excellent environment to grow, support to learning within working hours is a plus. open and friendly management, who cares about you and your career.
poor management. unfriendly, poor communication, no career progression help when asked several times, just a number. no work life balance. all take and no give. wouldn t recommend

gossip, immature senior leaders, corporately backwards. none. gossip, immature leaders, low pay for entry level employees, just a sense of doom daily. the old school carfinco employees and leaders not only have no other industry or workplace as a comparison, they act like they are in highschool. if you have any sense of professionalism or value trust, transparency and proper business practices do not apply here

stagnant. good place to work but not much growth opportunities. work life balance is dependent on manager and projects are ambiguous. need to be good with changing company management and be adaptable.

great environment for professional growth and delivery. santander is a great place to work with supportive leadership and collaborative, high - performing teams. the pm role offers strong development opportunities, exposure to complex programmes, and a culture that encourages growth, learning, and continuous improvement.
good people bad business. the people work hard but overall direction of business is cost cutting and outsourcing. not a long term career options if this scar you are looking for.
not a bad place. overall not a bad place to work. has one very far from what it once was, but management needs a course in how to properly treat their employees. 3 / 5 stars.

good benefits, pay and flexibility. what is the best part of working at the company? they have really good employee benefits, the pay is good and they are willing to discuss flexible working what is the most stressful part about working at the company? in the call centre the most stressful part is the targets, aht targets specifically what is the work environment and culture like at the company? it ' s generally a friendly office what is a typical day like for you at the company? taking inbound calls, some times of the year are busier than others so you can be constantly on calls.

a good starting point to get into banking, but the company seemed to be constantly scaling back resources, which for staff is very stressful. what is the best part of working at the company? exposure to banking & finance sector. what is the most stressful part about working at the company? micromanagement, hitting stats & targets, understaffed most of the time. what is the work environment and culture like at the company? target driven, the training is very rushed. what is a typical day like for you at the company? it is varied, cover many task & duties once trained.

they want you to do the work of three people. they want you to do the work of three people, but, when you ask about getting promoted or a raise it does not happen. a lot of people leaving at the same time and no hurry in hiring more.
had great co workers. management was terrible they overworked employees if you didnt have a big book younwere not valued they didnt care to give you work life balance terrible
good place to work. good place to work. you get the option to be remote or hybrid. if hybrid, the company does not pay for parking which can add up when going 3 times a week to the office.
good. great staff to work with but heavy workload for the wage, flexible with shift patterns and easy to get time off, regular coaching from management, systems and process could improve

great job just micro managed. i worked at santander for 2 years, it started out great until management was shuffled around. certain upper middle management would micromanage every move you make which made the job tedious but overall a good company to work for.

great manager, easy job, prefer part time! i had the best manager and i loved my job, it picked up quickly for me! if i was to work here again i would want to part - time because a full time schedule just personally isn t for me. there s no time to make personal appointments / take care of yourself and i love to do that!
good. enjoyable and good support and flexible home life balance training is thorough and feel like you have support when needed long term staff and managers

awesome company. what is the best part of working at the company? lived company the people manager etc what is the most stressful part about working at the company? nothing was stressful moment team was awesome what is the work environment and culture like at the company? friendly fun and was pleasant what is a typical day like for you at the company? productive busy. dialer all day

very stressful work & deceiving job offers but mostly good colleagues. it ' s a high - performance call centre with hours that will very quickly tire you out, with poor breaks but good pay. most staff are good but there are a few bad apples out there in the company in higher roles. onboarding process was nice but deceiving, don ' t let it fool you - it ' s still just a call centre, with the role being greatly different to how it was initial presented. not many make it past the training / onboarding stage.

good start up. i think it s a great place to get your self in the cooperate world, can learn a view things from some of the s3, my superiors was decent, but the job is definitely not paid well for the amount of work you had to do
ok place. ok place to work for, work life balance good benefits and great enviroment. lots if turnover but expected with the type of job. good to start a career

would not recommend. extremely micromanaged in this role. managers are very unsympathetic and unhelpful. you are spoken to awfully by the managers who work there, very patronising and not willing to help. do get a yearly bonus but they have made it more difficult for people to qualify. constantly changing the goalposts on what they want from you - whatever you do is not good enough.

unprofessional at times. overall a good company to work for, but branch staff you work with daily can be really unprofessional and only take things seriously when you have someone from the union backing you.

productive. hsbc offers a dynamic and multicultural work environment, especially appealing for those seeking international exposure. employees often highlight the company s strong emphasis on professional development, with access to learning platforms, mentorship programs, and global mobility opportunities. hsbc also promotes work - life balance through flexible working arrangements and wellness initiatives. the company s core values integrity, openness, and connection are reflected in its collaborative culture. however, some employees report challenges with bureaucratic processes and limited agility in decision - making due to its large corporate structure. career progression can be slow in certain departments, and restructuring efforts occasionally impact job security. overall, hsbc is regarded as a stable employer that values diversity, inclusion, and long - term growth, making it a suitable workplace for those aligned with the financial sector s evolving demands and a structured organizational environment.

comments given below. when i first joined, the work environment was positive and supportive, especially during the first two to three years. however, things started to shift significantly when management changed. the head of business performance began to dominate key decisions, and even the head of retail banking appeared to be influenced by him. this shift led to an increase in internal politics and favoritism. he brought in his own group of people, which disrupted the team dynamics and morale. despite working there for nearly six years, the overall experience was not as fulfilling as expected. i learned a lot in the early years, but the later period was challenging due to the culture shift and management style. the most enjoyable part was initially working in a collaborative team, but the hardest part became navigating the internal politics that eventually impacted performance and job satisfaction
good benefits. good benefits, good career start with comprehensive learning and development pla. culture is great as well. however, do expect politics but manageable.

hsbc review. working as a ui / ux designer at hsbc has been a rewarding experience. a typical day involves collaborating with product teams, conducting user research, and designing intuitive interfaces. i ve learned to balance user needs with business goals, and improved my design thinking and prototyping skills. management is encouraging, offering guidance while allowing creative freedom. the workplace culture promotes innovation, inclusivity, and continuous learning. the most challenging part is aligning stakeholder expectations with user - centric design, but it ' s also an opportunity for growth. the most enjoyable part is seeing designs come to life and positively impact millions of users.

awful. when i started at hsbc in 2018, i had a fantastic experience. supportive management, lots of development opportunities, loads of staff benefits and the flexibility was great. however, the culture has completely shifted since around 2022 and now hsbc is a terrible place to work and i wouldn t recommend it to anyone. most teams have some form of targets to meet and everything is monitored, regardless of the role you re in. they are now even tracking pass swipes to ensure office attendance and your end of year grade is impacted if you don t attend the office for at least 60 % of your time. micromanagement is the norm at hsbc, regardless of your team. you are very lucky if you find a supportive manager. all of their decisions are based off of statistics and they don ' t consider implications on staff, because they don ' t care about us. senior leaders boast about delivering fantastic outcomes and results, but when questioned on your staff are delivering for you so how are you going to deliver back to staff, the answer was we re not, if you don t like it, leave. what a terrible thing to say after all the hard work colleagues have put in to help the bank hit their targets. they are going backwards and are a very old school rigid corporate company. as well as monitoring office attendance, they have scrapped compressed hours across the company, decline the majority of flexible working requests, are cancelling work from home contracts, have changed the end of year grading policy to save money on bonuses, have extended business operating hours in most apartments meaning more colleagues are now working unsociable hours, then refused to give anything back to us for our hard work. it s a really toxic place to work and i can t wait to leave.

good salary and benefit, but no promotion. working as a universal banker at hsbc in hong kong has been a solid experience overall. the salary is competitive, especially compared to similar roles in other banks, and the benefits package is excellent including medical coverage, staff banking privileges, and generous annual leave. the work environment is fast - paced but professional, with well - structured processes and good training resources provided for new joiners. colleagues are generally supportive, and the bank maintains high standards for customer service. however, one major downside is the limited career advancement opportunities. promotion can be quite slow, and internal mobility is not as smooth as expected for such a large global institution. even high performers may find themselves in the same role for years with little clear path forward. pros : good salary and stable income excellent staff benefits and banking perks reputable international brand professional training and structured operations cons : limited promotion opportunities advancement can be slow despite good performance high - pressure sales targets at times conclusion : a great job for stability and compensation, but not ideal if you ' re looking for quick career progression.

fast growing. good people management is the foundation of a productive and positive workplace. it involves treating employees with respect, fairness, and empathy while aligning their strengths with organizational goals. effective managers communicate clearly, set realistic expectations, and provide regular feedback to help individuals grow. they recognize achievements, address challenges constructively, and foster an environment where people feel valued and motivated. good people management also means encouraging collaboration, listening actively, and being approachable so that team members feel comfortable sharing ideas or concerns. flexibility and adaptability are key understanding that different individuals have different working styles and needs. by building trust, supporting professional development, and promoting work - life balance, managers can boost engagement, reduce turnover, and drive better performance. ultimately, good people management is about creating a culture where employees feel empowered, supported, and inspired to contribute their best to the success of the organization.

productive. working as a financial analyst at pwc, one of the most globally recognized and prestigious professional services firms, offers a career experience that is simultaneously challenging, rewarding, and, at times, demanding in ways that require a high degree of adaptability, intellectual rigor, and emotional resilience, especially for those who are early in their professional journey or seeking to fast - track their development in the broader domain of finance, strategy, and consulting. from the moment you join pwc in this capacity, whether through campus recruitment or lateral hiring, you are immediately introduced to an environment that blends the fast - paced, metrics - driven culture of a large - scale consultancy with the structured, client - service orientation typical of a big four firm, where delivering value both in terms of financial insights and strategic recommendations is not just expected, but ingrained in the way the business operates across all verticals and geographies. your daily responsibilities, while sometimes repetitive in their technical foundations ( such as preparing financial reports, reconciling budget discrepancies, building forecast models, or contributing to the development of executive dashboards using tools like excel, power bi, or tableau ), are contextualized within a larger framework of business transformation, operational efficiency, and client impact, which means even the smallest analytical output can contribute to high - level decisions that affect the trajectory of multinational clients, internal finance operations, or strategic planning initiatives across the firm. one of the most compelling aspects of the role is the unparalleled learning curve, which is steep but highly rewarding for those who are willing to immerse themselves in the complexity of financial systems, understand the nuances of regulatory compliance, and continuously refine both hard skills ( such as quantitative modeling or variance analysis ) and soft skills ( like stakeholder communication and cross - functional collaboration ), often under tight deadlines and with evolving priorities that require not just time management but also a high level of intellectual agility.

amazing. working at pwc has been a truly valuable experience for my professional and personal growth. the firm offers a wide range of opportunities to learn and develop, whether through structured trainings, mentoring, or exposure to diverse industries and clients. i particularly appreciated the collaborative team culture and the chance to work with highly skilled colleagues who are always open to share knowledge and support. the international environment and strong brand name also make it a great place to build a career and expand your professional network. of course, the workload can be demanding at times, with tight deadlines and challenging projects, but this also pushes you to grow faster and develop resilience. pwc encourages innovation, adaptability, and continuous improvement, which creates an inspiring and dynamic workplace. overall, it is an excellent place to start or advance a career if you are motivated, curious, and eager to take on responsibility.

inventory counter. none. a typical day at work as an inventory counter at pwc involves checking stock levels, scanning items, verifying quantities against records, and assisting in organizing warehouse or storage areas. there s a strong emphasis on accuracy and attention to detail, which makes every day a learning opportunity. i believe i learned a lot about inventory management systems, proper handling of materials, and effective record - keeping. i also improved my organizational skills and ability to work efficiently under time constraints. management is approachable and supportive. supervisors are willing to answer questions and provide guidance, which helps a lot, especially when learning procedures for the first time. the workplace culture is collaborative and friendly. everyone is focused on accuracy and teamwork, and there s a positive environment for asking questions and learning from mistakes. the hardest part of the job is maintaining accuracy during high - volume inventory counts or under tight deadlines. it can be physically demanding at times, as it involves walking, lifting, and repetitive scanning.

good experience. pwc is a globally respected firm that offers strong exposure to high - quality clients, complex audits, and international reporting standards. working at pwc provides excellent technical training, particularly in ifrs, risk assessment, and audit methodology, which builds a solid foundation for a long - term career in finance or professional services. the firm s structured approach to learning, coaching, and performance management helps graduates and trainees develop discipline, attention to detail, and strong professional judgement. the people at pwc are generally supportive, driven, and collaborative, which creates a fast - paced but motivating environment. you are constantly challenged to improve, meet tight deadlines, and deliver work that meets global quality standards. while the workload can be intense, especially during busy season, the experience gained is extremely valuable and highly regarded in the market. overall, pwc is an excellent place to develop technical expertise, resilience, and confidence as a professional.
